title: validate-rules prints full Go stack traces to stderr on validation failure (use %v not %+v)
summary: When alert-rule validation fails, cos-tool prints the full Go stack trace to stderr (which then floods `juju debug-log`). Root cause is the `%+v` format verb applied to errors that come from `github.com/pkg/errors`: such errors implement a Formatter that, under `%+v`, renders the entire call stack. The offending lines are pkg/tool/promql_transform.go:26 and pkg/tool/logql_transform.go:22 -- both `return rg, fmt.Errorf("error validating %s: %+v", filename, errs)` -- and pkg/lokiruler/compat.go:23 `errs[i] = fmt.Errorf("%+v", errs[i])`, which wraps each Loki parse error with a stack-trace-bearing pkg/errors value. Switching `%+v` to `%v` (or `%s`) yields the concise one-line messages the README already advertises (e.g. 'error validating rule_file.yaml: [5:15: ...]').
why_good_first_issue: Single well-understood root cause with a tiny, low-risk diff (change `%+v` to `%v` in 2-3 lines). Maintainer-filed bug with a clear, user-visible symptom and an explicit 'expected behavior: user-friendly error messages without Go stacks'. The README at line 151-155 already shows the desired concise format, giving the contributor an exact target. Existing tests in pkg/tool validate the rule-error path so a contributor can extend one to assert the message no longer contains 'goroutine'/file paths.
repro_steps: Go is NOT installed on VM (command -v go empty), so inspection tier. On VM cos: multipass exec cos -- git clone --depth 80 https://github.com/canonical/cos-tool ~/work/cos-tool; then `grep -rn '%+v' pkg/ cmd/ main.go` and read pkg/tool/promql_transform.go:20-29, pkg/lokiruler/compat.go:11-26. Cross-reference the stack trace in issue #21 which names pkg/tool/promql_transform.go:14 (ValidateRules) and cmd/root/root.go:77.
repro_result: confirmed-by-inspection: `grep -rn '%+v'` returns exactly 3 hits -- pkg/tool/promql_transform.go:26, pkg/tool/logql_transform.go:22, pkg/lokiruler/compat.go:23. promql_transform.go:23-26 builds the error as `fmt.Errorf("error validating %s: %+v", filename, errs)` from rulefmt.Parse errors; compat.go imports `github.com/pkg/errors` (line 11) whose errors emit a stack trace under %+v. main.go:8-12 then does logrus `log.Fatal(err)` and root.go:84 returns `cli.Exit(err, 1)`, so the stack-laden string surfaces on stderr / juju debug-log exactly as in the issue. This is the precise mechanism behind the reported 'rulefmt.go:90 ... command.go:169 ...' trace.
fix_hint: Replace `%+v` with `%v` (or `%s`) in pkg/tool/promql_transform.go:26 and pkg/tool/logql_transform.go:22; in pkg/lokiruler/compat.go:23 drop the `fmt.Errorf("%+v", ...)` re-wrap entirely (or use `errors.Cause(errs[i])` / `%v`) so pkg/errors stack frames are not emitted. Add/extend a unit test in pkg/tool asserting the returned error string does not contain 'goroutine' or '.go:' line references. Optionally have main.go print to stderr without logrus's default formatting.
notes: Distinct, separate part of the issue: it also mentions a *duplicate group name* in generated rules (gft_model_..._alerts repeated) -- that is a charm-side rule-generation problem, NOT a cos-tool bug, so the cos-tool-scoped fix here is strictly the stack-trace formatting. Go absent so cannot `go test`; reproduction is inspection-tier with exact file:line + the issue's own stack trace as corroboration.

title: Add unit tests for untested packages (cmd/root and pkg/lokiruler are at 0% coverage)
suggested_labels: Type: Enhancement; good first issue; Status: Help Wanted
summary: The maintainer-filed issue lists several packages at 0.0% coverage. Excluding the vendored-from-Loki trees (pkg/logql/*), the genuinely cos-tool-owned untested code is the CLI wiring in cmd/root (cmd/root/root.go, 0.0%) and the Loki rule loader in pkg/lokiruler (pkg/lokiruler/compat.go, 0.0%). A beginner can add table-driven tests: for cmd/root, exercise the cli.App commands (transform / validate-rules / validate-config) and the Before hook that selects PromQL vs LogQL via the --format flag; for pkg/lokiruler, feed Load() valid and invalid rule YAML and assert the returned (*rulefmt.RuleGroups, []error).
why_good_first_issue: Pure additive work (no behaviour change), naturally scoped to a single package per PR, and Go's standard testing makes table-driven tests approachable. The repo already has good example tests in pkg/tool/*_test.go to copy the style from. Maintainer explicitly asked for this, so it is wanted and unlikely to be contested. Clear 'done': new _test.go files raising coverage for cmd/root and/or pkg/lokiruler above 0%.
repro_steps: Go is NOT installed on VM, so cannot run `go test ./... -coverprofile`. On VM cos: clone repo, then `find . -name '*_test.go' -not -path './pkg/logql/*'` (lists only pkg/tool/*) and `find cmd -name '*_test.go'` (empty) and `ls pkg/lokiruler` (only compat.go).
repro_result: confirmed-by-inspection: the only non-vendored test files are six files under pkg/tool/ (logql_transform_test.go, promql_alert_test.go, promql_transform_test.go, promql_config_check_test.go, logql_alert_test.go, common_test.go). `find cmd -name '*_test.go'` returns nothing -> cmd/root/root.go is untested (matches the issue's 'cmd/root coverage: 0.0%'). pkg/lokiruler/ contains only compat.go, no test -> matches 'lokiruler 0.0%'. So the issue's coverage gaps are still present on current main; it is NOT obsolete.
fix_hint: Start with the smallest target: add pkg/lokiruler/compat_test.go calling lokiruler.Load() on a small valid rule set (expect 0 errors) and on a malformed one (expect non-empty []error). Then cmd/root/root_test.go: build the cli.App (or call Execute with crafted os.Args via a helper) and assert transform output and validate-rules exit behaviour, including the --format promql|logql branch in the Before hook. Mirror the table-driven structure already used in pkg/tool/promql_transform_test.go.
notes: Scope guidance for the contributor: do NOT target the pkg/logql/* packages the issue also lists (jsonexpr, logfmt, pattern, syntax, util, logqlmodel) -- those are vendored copies of Grafana Loki internals and are not the right place for cos-tool's own tests; focus on cmd/root and pkg/lokiruler. Go absent so coverage numbers were verified structurally (presence/absence of _test.go) rather than by running `go test`.

title: Add log_level config option
summary: The k8s Grafana Agent charm hardcodes the agent server log level to 'info' in src/grafana_agent.py (_server_config: {"log_level": "info"}). The machine charm already added a user-facing `log_level` config option (canonical/grafana-agent-operator PR #217) accepting debug/info/warn/error and falling back to info on invalid input. This issue asks to bring the same option to the k8s charm for consistency.
why_good_first_issue: Small, well-scoped feature with an existing reference implementation to copy (machine charm PR #217). The render site is a single hardcoded line and the charm already reads other options via self.config[...]. Clear definition of done: add the option in charmcraft.yaml, wire it into _server_config with validation+fallback, add a unit/scenario test. No deep architecture knowledge required.
affected_files: charmcraft.yaml (options block, ~line 125); src/grafana_agent.py:896 (hardcoded log_level); tests/unit or tests/scenario
repro_steps: multipass exec cos -- git clone --depth 80 https://github.com/canonical/grafana-agent-k8s-operator ~/work/grafana-agent-k8s-operator; multipass exec cos -- bash -lc 'cd ~/work/grafana-agent-k8s-operator && export PATH=$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH && tox -e unit'; prove the gap: grep -n 'log_level' charmcraft.yaml (none) and sed -n '890,900p' src/grafana_agent.py (hardcoded).
repro_result: confirmed-by-inspection + green baseline: `tox -e unit` => unit: OK (28.8s), TOTAL coverage 78% (suite passes). charmcraft.yaml options block (tls_insecure_skip_verify, always_enable_*, tracing_sample_rate_*) has NO log_level option. src/grafana_agent.py:896 hardcodes server_config = {"log_level": "info"} with no way to override. Reference impl exists: canonical/grafana-agent-operator PR #217 (levels debug/info/warn/error, invalid -> info + WARNING).
fix_hint: Add to charmcraft.yaml options: log_level (string, default 'info', describe allowed values debug/info/warn/error). In src/grafana_agent.py _server_config, replace the literal 'info' with a validated read of self.config['log_level'] (lowercase, validate against {debug,info,warn,error}, fall back to 'info' and logger.warning on invalid), mirroring grafana-agent-operator PR #217. Add a test setting log_level=error and asserting it lands in the rendered server config.
notes: Issue is Type: Enhancement + Status: Triage but is genuinely beginner-friendly with a concrete reference PR. Verified the related #131 (positions_dir) is already addressed (charm.py:261 returns '/run') and #230 (tox pollution) is effectively obsolete (post-`tox -e unit` git status is clean; the 3 alert/dashboard dirs are gitignored and not created at repo root), so neither was picked.

title: Copy-paste docstring and wrong NotImplementedError message in GrafanaAgentCharm abstract methods
summary: In the abstract-methods section of src/grafana_agent.py (base class GrafanaAgentCharm), two stale copy-paste defects exist: (1) `_additional_log_configs` has the docstring 'Additional per-type integrations to inject.' which is identical to `_additional_integrations` above it and does not describe log configs; (2) `is_command_changed` raises NotImplementedError('Please override the command method') naming the wrong method ('command' instead of 'is_command_changed'), unlike every other abstract method which names itself correctly.
why_good_first_issue: Pure documentation/accuracy cleanup with an unambiguous correct form derived from the surrounding code. No behavior change, no architecture knowledge needed. Each sibling abstract method provides the correct pattern to follow. Clear done: docstring describes log configs; error message names is_command_changed.
repro_steps: multipass exec cos -- bash -lc 'cd ~/work/grafana-agent-k8s-operator && sed -n "525,567p" src/grafana_agent.py'; grep -n 'Additional per-type integrations to inject\|Please override the command method' src/grafana_agent.py
repro_result: confirmed-by-inspection: src/grafana_agent.py line 532 (_additional_integrations) and line 537 (_additional_log_configs) carry the IDENTICAL docstring 'Additional per-type integrations to inject.'; the 537 copy is wrong for a log-configs method. Line 563: is_command_changed (defined line 561) raises NotImplementedError('Please override the command method') -- wrong method name; all sibling abstract methods (restart->'restart method', metrics_jobs->'metrics_jobs method', etc.) correctly name themselves. Subclass overrides exist (charm.py:116 is_command_changed, charm.py:198 _additional_log_configs) so these are the developer-facing contract.
fix_hint: Change the line 537 docstring to something like 'Additional per-type log configs to inject.'; change the line 563 message to 'Please override the is_command_changed method'. Optional: run `tox -e lint` to confirm no regressions (text-only change).
notes: Self-identified because the open issues are mostly obsolete/vague: #230 effectively fixed, #131 already addressed, #41 (config comments) is Priority: Low and underspecified (yaml.safe_dump output has no comment support without ruamel). This cleanup is a cleaner, verifiable beginner task. Unit suite green (28 tests OK).

title: The exception handling in `_update_config` is wrong.
summary: In _update_config, after writing the config the code calls self.restart() and wraps it in `except GrafanaAgentReloadError` and `except APIError`. But for this machine (snap) charm restart() raises GrafanaAgentServiceError, not GrafanaAgentReloadError; APIError is a Pebble exception left over from the k8s charm and can never be raised here. So a real restart failure is not caught (and even if it were, BlockedStatus is wrong since the user cannot fix it - it should surface as an error).
why_good_first_issue: Small, precisely-described bug confined to one method; the issue body enumerates exactly the three things to change; fixable without architectural knowledge; very low existing test coverage of this path (only tests/unit/test_cert_transfer.py touches restart) so adding a focused unit test is a natural, self-contained deliverable.
repro_steps: On VM cos: git clone --depth 80 into ~/work; `sed -n '642,654p' src/grafana_agent.py` to read the try/except; `grep -n 'def restart' src/grafana_agent.py src/charm.py` and read the concrete restart() to see which exception it raises; `tox -l` confirms a `unit` env exists for adding a regression test.
repro_result: Confirmed on HEAD ad6f720. src/grafana_agent.py:644 calls `self.restart()`; src/grafana_agent.py:647 `except GrafanaAgentReloadError as e:` -> BlockedStatus; src/grafana_agent.py:650 `except APIError as e:` -> WaitingStatus. The base restart() (src/grafana_agent.py:405) is abstract; the concrete override raises GrafanaAgentServiceError (src/charm.py:431 and :438 'Failed to restart grafana-agent'). Therefore the caught exception types do not match what restart() actually raises -> a restart failure propagates uncaught. APIError is imported from ops.pebble (src/grafana_agent.py:40), a Pebble-only error, confirming it is dead handling in this snap charm. Code search shows no unit test exercises this except path.
fix_hint: In _update_config replace `except GrafanaAgentReloadError` with `except GrafanaAgentServiceError`, remove the `except APIError` clause (and drop the now-unused ops.pebble.APIError import if nothing else uses it), and surface the failure as an error/raise rather than BlockedStatus since it is not user-actionable. Add a unit test that makes restart() raise GrafanaAgentServiceError and asserts the resulting status/behaviour.
notes: Existing OPEN Type: Bug issue, still reproducible. The buggy block moved from src/charm.py (as quoted in the issue) to src/grafana_agent.py during refactor but is byte-for-byte the same logic, so the issue is current. Note the leftover FIXME on line 645 referencing #19.

title: Docs for refresh_events is incorrect
summary: The cos_agent.py library docstrings show `refresh_events=["update-status", "upgrade-charm"]` (string event names) as the usage example, and COSAgentRequirer type-hints refresh_events as Optional[List[str]]. But the code observes each entry via framework.observe, which requires BoundEvent objects (e.g. self.on.config_changed), so the documented string form raises `RuntimeError: Framework.observe requires a BoundEvent as second parameter`. The docs (prose, example, and type hint) should show event classes, and should note the default is on.config_changed.
why_good_first_issue: Pure documentation/type-hint fix in one library file; no behavioural change; the issue and a follow-up comment spell out exactly what to correct (examples, type hints, and document the config_changed default); ideal for a newcomer learning the ops event model.
repro_steps: On VM cos: git clone --depth 80 into ~/work; `grep -n 'refresh_events' lib/charms/grafana_agent/v0/cos_agent.py`; `sed -n '105,120p'` and `sed -n '958,968p'` to read the example and the type hint; `sed -n '673,682p'` to see the observe loop that requires BoundEvents.
repro_result: Confirmed on HEAD ad6f720. Docstring examples at cos_agent.py:114 and cos_agent.py:209 both show `refresh_events=["update-status", "upgrade-charm"]` (strings). COSAgentRequirer.__init__ type hint at cos_agent.py:964 is `refresh_events: Optional[List[str]] = None`. The init then does (cos_agent.py:680) a loop `for event in self._refresh_events: ... framework.observe(event, ...)`, and self._refresh_events defaults to `[self._charm.on.config_changed]` (cos_agent.py:673) - i.e. BoundEvents, not strings. So the documented string usage would crash exactly as the issue's RuntimeError shows. Observed: docs say strings; expected: event classes (and document the config_changed default).
fix_hint: Update both docstring examples to use event classes, e.g. `refresh_events=[self.on.update_status, self.on.config_changed]`; change the COSAgentRequirer type hint on cos_agent.py:964 from Optional[List[str]] to match the BoundEvent usage (consistent with the Optional[List] used elsewhere at line 39/631); add a sentence noting refresh_events defaults to on.config_changed. Bump LIBPATCH per charm-library convention.
notes: Existing OPEN issue (no Type label upstream but clearly a documentation bug). A maintainer-adjacent comment additionally requests documenting that refresh_events defaults to on.config_changed - captured in the fix hint. Verified still wrong on HEAD.

title: goss: add a check that the shipped 'agent' alias binary resolves and runs
suggested_labels: Type: Enhancement; good first issue; testing
summary: rockcraft.yaml deliberately ships two binaries: /usr/bin/grafana-agent and a /usr/bin/agent alias (created via 'ln -sv /bin/grafana-agent' then organized to usr/bin/agent). The shared root goss.yaml checks the running process and three HTTP endpoints but never verifies that the 'agent' alias actually exists and is runnable, even though the alias is a deliberate, rock-specific addition that could silently break. A beginner can add a file/command check for /usr/bin/agent.
why_good_first_issue: Pure declarative YAML change in goss.yaml; the rock-specific alias is the most likely thing to regress and is currently untested. Clear done-state: a goss file/command check asserting /usr/bin/agent exists and 'agent --version' succeeds. No build knowledge required; validated by 'just test' (kgoss).
repro_steps: gh api repos/canonical/grafana-agent-rock/contents/0.44.8/rockcraft.yaml --jq .content | base64 -d | grep -nE 'ln -sv|usr/bin/agent'  (shows the agent alias is created and organized to usr/bin/agent); gh api repos/canonical/grafana-agent-rock/contents/goss.yaml --jq .content | base64 -d  (no file/command check for either binary).
repro_result: confirmed-by-inspection: 0.44.8/rockcraft.yaml line 34 'ln -sv /bin/grafana-agent .../opt/agent/bin/agent' + organize 'opt/agent/bin/agent: usr/bin/agent' ship a /usr/bin/agent alias; goss.yaml only has 'process: grafana-agent: running' and three http checks (instances, /-/ready, /-/healthy) with no file: or command: check for /usr/bin/agent (or /usr/bin/grafana-agent).
fix_hint: In goss.yaml add: command: 'agent --version': {exit-status: 0} and/or file: /usr/bin/agent: {exists: true, filetype: symlink, linked-to: /bin/grafana-agent}. Validate with 'just test'.
notes: No version-bump available: 0.44.8/ already pins source-tag v0.44.8, which is the newest upstream tag (grafana/agent tags top out at v0.44.8; the releases/latest API is stale at v0.44.3). README already documents EOL by end of 2026 (migrate to opentelemetry-collector), so deprecation is not an open task.

title: goss: assert response body / content on the agent HTTP health and instances endpoints
suggested_labels: Type: Enhancement; good first issue; testing
summary: The root goss.yaml verifies that three HTTP endpoints return status 200 (/agent/api/v1/metrics/instances, /-/ready, /-/healthy) but never asserts anything about the response bodies. A 200 with empty or error JSON would still pass. A beginner can add 'body' regex assertions (e.g. the /-/healthy endpoint should report the agent is healthy) so the smoke test catches more subtle regressions.
why_good_first_issue: Small, declarative addition to an existing goss check block; the endpoints and expected payloads are easy to discover by curling a running rock. Good introduction to goss 'body' matchers without touching the rockcraft build. Done-state: at least one endpoint has a body assertion and 'just test' passes.
repro_steps: gh api repos/canonical/grafana-agent-rock/contents/goss.yaml --jq .content | base64 -d  (each http entry has only 'status: 200' and 'url:', no 'body:' key).
repro_result: confirmed-by-inspection: all three goss http checks (instances, ready, healthy) specify only status: 200 and a url; none has a body: matcher, so a 200 response with unexpected content would still pass the smoke test.
fix_hint: Add 'body: ["..."]' regex matchers under the existing http entries in goss.yaml (e.g. the /-/healthy body, which upstream typically returns as a short OK/healthy string). Confirm the exact strings by curling a packed rock, then validate with 'just test'.
notes: Secondary to the 'agent alias' goss check; both are isolated edits to the same shared goss.yaml, which covers all version directories at once. Only open issue (#116) is an auto-generated vulnerability-scan report, not a beginner task.

title: Uncaught `FileNotFoundError` in `grafana_version()` when pebble socket closes during pod termination
summary: grafana_version() in src/grafana.py is a TOCTOU race: it guards with self._container.can_connect() (line 92) then calls self._container.exec([...]).wait_output() (line 94) with no exception handling. If the pebble socket closes between the check and the exec (pod termination / SIGTERM), a FileNotFoundError propagates uncaught and puts the unit in error state during config-changed/_reconcile.
why_good_first_issue: Single self-contained method, exact file:line given, and the fix pattern already exists elsewhere in the same file (other methods catch FileNotFoundError/ConnectionError). No architectural knowledge needed; just wrap the exec/wait_output in try/except and return "" (matching the existing not-connected branch).
repro_steps: multipass exec cos -- bash -lc 'cd ~/work/grafana-k8s-operator && sed -n "86,100p" src/grafana.py' ; observe can_connect() guard at line 92 immediately followed by unguarded exec(...).wait_output() at line 94. Compare with line 524 (`except (FileNotFoundError, ProtocolError, PathError)`) and imports at lines 25-31 which already import ConnectionError/APIError, confirming the codebase already handles these elsewhere.
repro_result: Confirmed by inspection: src/grafana.py line 92 `if not self._container.can_connect(): return ""` then line 94 `version_output, _ = self._container.exec(["grafana-server", "-v"]).wait_output()` has NO surrounding try/except. Observed: a FileNotFoundError/ConnectionError raised by exec() after a successful can_connect() check is uncaught. Expected: it should be caught and return "" like the not-connected case. Live race (pod-termination SIGTERM between check and exec) cannot be triggered without mutating the cos model, but the missing guard is unambiguous in code.
fix_hint: Wrap the exec/wait_output call in try/except catching the pebble/OS errors already imported (ConnectionError, APIError) plus FileNotFoundError, returning "" on failure (same as the can_connect() False branch). Mirror the existing handling at src/grafana.py:524.
notes: Issue auto-filed by solutions-qa from log analysis; traceback in body points exactly at grafana.py:94 and charm.py _reconcile -> set_workload_version. Unlabelled in tracker but textbook good-first-issue.

title: Incorrect cosl version dependency in grafana_dashboard library
summary: lib/charms/grafana_k8s/v0/grafana_dashboard.py declares PYDEPS = ["cosl >= 0.0.50"] (line 222) but imports `from cosl.types import type_convert_stored` (line 192) and `from cosl import DashboardPath40UID, LZMABase64` (line 191). type_convert_stored / DashboardPath40UID are only available in newer cosl, so a consuming charm that pins an old cosl per PYDEPS will hit ImportError at import time.
why_good_first_issue: One-line metadata fix in a clearly-bounded library: bump the PYDEPS lower bound to the cosl version that actually provides cosl.types.type_convert_stored and cosl.DashboardPath40UID, then bump LIBPATCH. No logic changes.
repro_steps: multipass exec cos -- bash -lc 'cd ~/work/grafana-k8s-operator && grep -n "cosl\|PYDEPS\|type_convert_stored\|DashboardPath40UID\|LIBPATCH" lib/charms/grafana_k8s/v0/grafana_dashboard.py | head'
repro_result: Confirmed by inspection: line 191 `from cosl import DashboardPath40UID, LZMABase64`, line 192 `from cosl.types import type_convert_stored`, but line 222 `PYDEPS = ["cosl >= 0.0.50"]`. Observed: declared dependency floor (0.0.50) is far below the cosl version that introduced cosl.types.type_convert_stored / DashboardPath40UID. A charm honouring PYDEPS with an old cosl gets ImportError. (Note: current floor 0.0.50 is even lower than the >=0.5.0 quoted in the issue body, so the mismatch is real and arguably worse than reported.) Expected: PYDEPS floor >= the cosl release providing those symbols.
fix_hint: Raise PYDEPS lower bound to the cosl release that first ships cosl.types.type_convert_stored and cosl.DashboardPath40UID (issue cites ~1.3.0; verify against cosl changelog), then bump LIBPATCH (line 220) since lib metadata changed.
notes: Library-internal fix; affects downstream charms that vendor this lib. Verify the precise cosl version against the cosl package before finalising the bound.

title: Update the `grafana-ui` part build recipe to be closer to upstream
summary: The grafana-ui part in rockcraft.yaml cannot run upstream's recommended 'yarn install --immutable' because, inside LXD, yarn 4 fails with EINVAL when a dependency is pinned to a git commit in yarn.lock (e.g. react-data-grid pinned to a commit). The current workaround sed-rewrites that one pinned dep to a tarball URL before 'yarn install'. The issue asks to make the recipe more robust/closer to upstream so it does not break the next time a different dep is pinned to a git commit.
why_good_first_issue: Maintainer-filed, scoped to a single override-build block in one file, with reproduction steps, the exact error, and links to the upstream yarn/LXD bugs. A newcomer comfortable with yarn/Node can generalize the sed hack (or adjust yarn config / use the upstream tarball flow). Honest caveat: root cause touches an LXD kernel-feature discrepancy, so a full fix may be involved; difficulty marked medium rather than trivially easy.
affected_files: 12.4.2/rockcraft.yaml (grafana-ui part override-build); same block in newer version dirs (11.x/12.x)
repro_steps: gh issue view 94 -R canonical/grafana-rock; gh api repos/canonical/grafana-rock/contents/12.4.2/rockcraft.yaml --jq .content | base64 -d | sed -n '36,70p'  (shows the commented-out 'yarn install --immutable' and the sed workaround that rewrites grafana/react-data-grid#<commit> to a github archive tarball before 'yarn install').
repro_result: confirmed-by-inspection: 12.4.2/rockcraft.yaml grafana-ui part comments out '#yarn install --immutable' and instead hardcodes COMMIT=a10c748f40edc538425b458af4e471a8262ec4ed plus a sed rewrite of grafana/react-data-grid#${COMMIT} to a tarball URL, then runs 'yarn install'. This matches issue #94's description that the immutable install fails under LXD on git-commit-pinned deps and the workaround only handles a single pinned dep.
fix_hint: Generalize the rewrite to handle ANY 'github.com/<org>/<repo>#commit=<sha>' or '...#<sha>' entry in yarn.lock/package.json (loop instead of a single hardcoded COMMIT), or switch to yarn's supportedArchitectures/npmRegistryServer config, or follow upstream's tarball-resolution flow. Validate by packing in a Multipass VM (issue notes Multipass succeeds where LXD fails).
notes: Real maintainer issue (no label, 0 comments, unclaimed). Related upstream bugs linked in the issue: lxd#18014 and yarnpkg/berry#3500. The same grafana-ui recipe is duplicated across version dirs, so a robust fix would ideally be applied to the newest ones. Other 3 open issues are auto-generated vulnerability-scan reports.

title: Bump Grafana rock to upstream v13.0.2 (newest dir pins v12.4.2)
summary: The newest version directory in the repo is 12.4.2/, whose rockcraft.yaml builds Grafana from source-tag v12.4.2. Upstream grafana/grafana has since released the v13.x line, with v13.0.2 as the latest stable. A beginner can add a new version directory copying the 12.4.2 layout and bumping version and the two source-tag references to v13.0.2, following the repo's established per-version pattern.
why_good_first_issue: Mechanical change matching an existing pattern (16 version dirs from 9.5.3 to 12.4.2). Mostly copy-the-latest-dir and change version strings; CI/kgoss validates. Definition of done is clear. NOTE: the grafana-ui build (issue #94) may need attention for v13, so the bumper should expect to also adjust the yarn workaround.
repro_steps: gh api repos/canonical/grafana-rock/contents --jq '.[]|select(.type=="dir")|.name'  (newest = 12.4.2); gh api repos/canonical/grafana-rock/contents/12.4.2/rockcraft.yaml --jq .content | base64 -d | grep -E 'version:|source-tag:'; gh api repos/grafana/grafana/releases/latest --jq .tag_name (and tags list).
repro_result: confirmed-by-inspection: 12.4.2/rockcraft.yaml pins version: "12.4.2" and source-tag: v12.4.2 (twice: grafana and grafana-ui parts), and 12.4.2 is the newest version directory; upstream grafana/grafana releases/latest is v13.0.2 (stable tags include v13.0.0/1/2 and v12.4.3/12.4.4), so the rock trails the latest upstream by a major version.
fix_hint: Copy 12.4.2/ to 13.0.2/; set version: "13.0.2" and both source-tag references to v13.0.2; keep base ubuntu@26.04 and license AGPL-3.0. Be prepared to update the grafana-ui yarn workaround per issue #94 since v12+ is where the git-commit-pinned-dep problem appears. A smaller interim bump to v12.4.4 (same minor line) is also possible. Validate with CI/kgoss.
notes: An update.yaml automation exists to raise version-bump PRs, but the newest dir is still 12.4.2 while v13 is out. This bump is coupled to issue #94 (grafana-ui recipe), so it is slightly less trivial than a pure tag bump.

title: config-changed hook crashes with unhandled 404 when Kubernetes Gateway API CRDs are absent
summary: On config-changed, the charm reconciles waypoint resources via KubernetesResourceManager.reconcile(), which lists existing labelled gateway.networking.k8s.io/v1 objects to compute a delta. When the Kubernetes Gateway API CRDs are not installed, that list raises an uncaught httpx.HTTPStatusError 404 and the charm goes to error state (retrying every few minutes) instead of a friendly blocked status like 'Kubernetes Gateway API CRDs not available'.
why_good_first_issue: Clear, well-scoped bug with an exact error/traceback supplied by QA, and an obvious correct behaviour (catch the missing-CRD 404 and set BlockedStatus). The codebase already has the pattern to imitate: _is_waypoint_deployment_ready catches lightkube ApiError at src/charm.py:306. A beginner adds a guard around the gateway reconcile path. Slightly above trivial only because faithful repro needs a cluster without the Gateway CRDs.
affected_files: src/charm.py:490 (krm.reconcile(resources_list) in _sync_waypoint_resources) and src/charm.py:461 (prm.reconcile) reached from _sync_all_resources (src/charm.py:318/335); gateway resource def src/charm.py:69-70; no CRD/404 guard exists anywhere in src/charm.py
repro_steps: PLAN (do NOT run on the live cos VM): 1) On a cluster WITHOUT the Gateway API CRDs (no gateway.networking.k8s.io), juju deploy istio-beacon-k8s --trust (do not deploy istio-k8s which would install the CRDs). 2) Trigger config-changed (e.g. juju config istio-beacon-k8s ...). 3) Observe juju debug-log: 'httpx.HTTPStatusError: Client error 404 Not Found for url .../apis/gateway.networking.k8s.io/v1/gateways?labelSelector=...kubernetes-resource-handler-scope=istio-waypoint' and the unit in error state. Expected after fix: BlockedStatus 'Kubernetes Gateway API CRDs not available'.
repro_result: LIVE-REPRODUCED 2026-06-16 (cos VM, throwaway model, no Gateway CRDs): deploy istio-beacon-k8s 1/edge --trust -> hook failed config-changed; httpx 404 on /apis/gateway.networking.k8s.io/v1/gateways, unhandled in _sync_waypoint_resources. || needs-live-deploy (flagged for orchestrator). Static evidence confirms the bug is present and unfixed: grep for 'CRD|404|HTTPStatusError|status_code|not available' in src/charm.py returns NONE -> no missing-CRD handling exists. The unguarded path is src/charm.py:490 krm.reconcile(resources_list) (KubernetesResourceManager lists gateway.networking.k8s.io/v1 to diff), called from _sync_waypoint_resources <- _sync_all_resources (config-changed handler). The only ApiError catch (src/charm.py:306) guards the Deployment .get, not the gateway list, matching the 404-on-gateways URL in the issue. Baseline tox -e unit PASSED (exit 0).
fix_hint: Wrap the gateway-listing reconcile (krm.reconcile in _sync_waypoint_resources, and likely prm.reconcile) in try/except for lightkube ApiError / httpx.HTTPStatusError; when status_code == 404 for the Gateway API group, set ops.BlockedStatus('Kubernetes Gateway API CRDs not available') and return instead of propagating. Reuse the existing ApiError-handling style at src/charm.py:306. Add a unit test using the scenario/harness mock to simulate the 404.
notes: Real open bug filed by canonical/solutions-qa against rev 34 / channel 1/stable; manual triage but the traceback is genuine. Integration-escalation: faithful repro requires a cluster lacking the Gateway CRDs; the shared live cos model must not be used.

title: Charm can go to active without metrics-proxy being alive
summary: _sync_all_resources() calls _setup_proxy_pebble_service() and then unconditionally sets ActiveStatus(). But _setup_proxy_pebble_service() returns early (does nothing) when the metrics-proxy container cannot connect, and swallows ChangeError on replan. So the charm reports Active even when the metrics-proxy Pebble service was never set up / started.
why_good_first_issue: Small, localized status-logic fix in a single method with an unambiguous DoD ('Active only if metrics-proxy is working'). The existing code already exposes the can_connect()/replan failure points, so the fix is to surface them as WaitingStatus/BlockedStatus instead of silently continuing. Easily unit-testable with the existing scenario tests.
affected_files: src/charm.py:318-344 (_sync_all_resources sets ActiveStatus at line 344 after line 339 call); src/charm.py:172-198 (_setup_proxy_pebble_service: early return at line 176 when !can_connect, ChangeError swallowed at ~line 197)
repro_steps: PLAN (do NOT run on the live cos VM): 1) Deploy istio-beacon-k8s on a mesh model. 2) Make the metrics-proxy container unavailable / its pebble socket unreachable (e.g. before the workload container is up, or kill the proxy service) so proxy_container.can_connect() is False during config-changed. 3) Observe juju status reports active even though `juju exec --unit istio-beacon-k8s/0 -- PEBBLE_SOCKET=... /charm/bin/pebble services` shows metrics-proxy not running. Expected after fix: status is Waiting/Blocked, not Active.
repro_result: LIVE-ASSESSED 2026-06-16: not force-reproduced (needs istio installed + manually stopping the metrics-proxy pebble service; avoided repeat istio cluster-webhook churn on the shared cos node). Code-confirmed. || needs-live-deploy (flagged for orchestrator). Confirmed by code inspection that the defect is present: src/charm.py:339 calls self._setup_proxy_pebble_service(); src/charm.py:344 sets self.unit.status = ActiveStatus() with no check of proxy health in between. _setup_proxy_pebble_service (src/charm.py:172) returns at src/charm.py:176 ('if not proxy_container.can_connect(): return') without signalling failure, and the replan ChangeError is only logged (src/charm.py:196-198), so a failed/absent metrics-proxy still yields ActiveStatus. (Issue text says the method 'can return False'; the code has since drifted to return None, but the root cause -- no status check after setup -- is unchanged.) Baseline tox -e unit PASSED (exit 0).
fix_hint: Make _setup_proxy_pebble_service return a bool (or raise) indicating whether the service is up (False on !can_connect and on ChangeError), then in _sync_all_resources gate ActiveStatus on it: if proxy not ready, set ops.WaitingStatus('metrics-proxy not ready') and return before ActiveStatus. Add/extend a scenario unit test asserting non-Active status when the metrics-proxy container cannot connect.
notes: Real open Type: Bug with a precise file:line pointer in the issue. Minor drift: _setup_proxy_pebble_service now returns None rather than False, but the missing post-setup status check remains the bug. Repro tier is integration because asserting the runtime status mismatch needs a deployed unit with a broken proxy container; the static evidence above is exact.

title: charm goes to error with unhelpful message if deployed somewhere without istio
summary: When istio-ingress-k8s is deployed on a cluster without istio (no Gateway API / istio CRDs), every hook runs _sync_all_resources(), which calls several KubernetesResourceManager.reconcile() paths (auth policies and gateway resources) that list gateway.networking.k8s.io/v1 objects. With the CRDs absent this raises an uncaught httpx.HTTPStatusError (404) and the unit goes to error with a cryptic traceback. The charm even has a friendly message ready (BlockedStatus 'Gateway k8s deployment not ready, is istio properly installed?' at src/charm.py:1147) but it lives in _on_collect_status, which never runs because the hook crashes first.
why_good_first_issue: Clear UX bug with an obvious DoD: detect missing istio/Gateway CRDs and surface a helpful BlockedStatus instead of crashing. The repo already establishes the pattern (ApiError caught at src/charm.py:566,590; intended message text already authored at src/charm.py:1147), so the change is to add an early CRD/availability check or a try/except around the reconcile calls. Above trivial only because faithful end-to-end repro needs a cluster without istio.
affected_files: src/charm.py:948 (_sync_all_resources, the hook entrypoint); src/charm.py:922,946 (auth-policy reconcile), src/charm.py:1031 (_sync_gateway_resources) and src/charm.py:1384/1609 (gateway/route reconcile) -- all run before the _is_ready() guard at src/charm.py:1032; intended-but-unreachable message at src/charm.py:1147
repro_steps: PLAN (do NOT run on the live cos VM): 1) On a clean cluster with NO istio installed (no gateway.networking.k8s.io CRDs; do not deploy istio-k8s), juju deploy istio-ingress-k8s --trust. 2) Observe the unit go to error; juju debug-log shows an uncaught httpx.HTTPStatusError 404 for .../apis/gateway.networking.k8s.io/v1/gateways?labelSelector=... raised from a *.reconcile() call inside _sync_all_resources. Expected after fix: BlockedStatus with a clear 'istio not installed / Gateway API CRDs not available' message and no traceback.
repro_result: LIVE-REPRODUCED 2026-06-16: deploy istio-ingress-k8s 1/edge --trust, no istio -> hook failed leader-elected; httpx 404 on /apis/security.istio.io/v1/authorizationpolicies in _sync_ext_authz_auth_policy, before the readiness guard. || needs-live-deploy (flagged for orchestrator). Static evidence confirms the gap: every hook handler (e.g. _on_config_changed src/charm.py:453, _on_start src/charm.py:457) calls _sync_all_resources (src/charm.py:948); inside it the reconcile() calls at src/charm.py:922/946/1031 (which list gateway.networking.k8s.io resources) execute BEFORE the _is_ready() guard at src/charm.py:1032 and before _on_collect_status (src/charm.py:1082). The only friendly message ('is istio properly installed?', src/charm.py:1147) is in collect-status and is therefore unreachable when the reconcile throws. grep for 'CRD|not installed|not available' in src/charm.py finds no pre-flight CRD check. Baseline tox -e unit PASSED (unit: OK, exit 0, 87% cov).
fix_hint: Add a pre-flight check at the top of _sync_all_resources (or wrap the reconcile calls) that detects the missing Gateway API / istio CRDs -- e.g. catch lightkube ApiError / httpx.HTTPStatusError and inspect status_code (404 == CRD absent) -- and set ops.BlockedStatus('Istio is not installed on this cluster (Gateway API CRDs missing)') then return, rather than propagating. Add a scenario unit test simulating the 404 to assert the BlockedStatus.
notes: Real open Type: Bug. Root cause is shared with issue #34 (same gateway-list call; #34 is the 403/RBAC variant). Integration-escalation: faithful repro needs a cluster without istio; the shared live cos model must not be used.

title: Missing RBAC permission when deploying istio-ingress-k8s charm
summary: Deploying istio-ingress-k8s can fail with an uncaught httpx.HTTPStatusError 403 Forbidden on GET .../apis/gateway.networking.k8s.io/v1/gateways?labelSelector=...kubernetes-resource-handler-scope=istio-gateway. The charm's service account lacks RBAC to list gateways (and likely other gateway.networking.k8s.io resources) at the needed scope, so the reconcile in _sync_all_resources crashes the hook with a traceback instead of failing gracefully.
why_good_first_issue: Well-scoped: the failing verb/resource (list gateways in gateway.networking.k8s.io) is pinned by the traceback, so a beginner can add the missing RBAC rule (and/or a graceful 403 handler with a clear BlockedStatus). Mirrors the already-understood istio-k8s issue #38. Above trivial because verifying the RBAC fix needs a real (often Charmed-Kubernetes / restricted-RBAC) cluster.
affected_files: src/charm.py:922/946/1031/1384/1609 (the *.reconcile() calls that list gateway.networking.k8s.io resources) reached from _sync_all_resources (src/charm.py:948); charmcraft.yaml (charm RBAC / trust expectations); ApiError handlers at src/charm.py:566,590 do not cover these reconcile calls
repro_steps: PLAN (do NOT run on the live cos VM): 1) On a cluster with restrictive RBAC (e.g. Charmed Kubernetes, where the istio-ingress-k8s service account is not granted list/get on gateway.networking.k8s.io), juju deploy istio-ingress-k8s --trust. 2) Observe unit error; juju debug-log shows 'HTTP/1.1 403 Forbidden' for .../gateway.networking.k8s.io/v1/gateways?labelSelector=... and an uncaught httpx.HTTPStatusError from main(IstioIngressCharm). 3) Confirm fix by granting the ClusterRole verbs (get/list/watch on gateways etc.) and re-deploying; unit should progress past the reconcile.
repro_result: LIVE-ATTEMPTED 2026-06-16: NOT reproducible on microk8s (istio-ingress-k8s deployed without --trust still went active; microk8s grants enough cluster perms). Needs Charmed Kubernetes' stricter RBAC. || needs-live-deploy (flagged for orchestrator). Could not reproduce locally: the repo is read-only and the shared cos VM's MicroK8s has permissive RBAC, so the 403 does not occur there; the bug manifests on restricted-RBAC clusters per the issue traceback. Static evidence: the crashing call is one of the *.reconcile() calls (src/charm.py:922/946/1031/1384/1609) which list gateway.networking.k8s.io objects with the exact labelSelector 'kubernetes-resource-handler-scope=istio-gateway' shown in the issue, executed from _sync_all_resources (src/charm.py:948) on every hook, with no 403/RBAC handling (ApiError catches at src/charm.py:566,590 cover only Deployment/Service .get). Baseline tox -e unit PASSED (unit: OK, exit 0).
fix_hint: Two complementary parts: (a) add the missing RBAC -- a ClusterRole granting get/list/watch (and create/patch/delete as needed) on gateway.networking.k8s.io resources -- bound to the charm's service account; (b) optionally catch the 403 in the reconcile path and set ops.BlockedStatus('Insufficient RBAC permissions; deploy with --trust / required ClusterRole') instead of letting it crash. Cross-reference the sibling fix for istio-k8s #38.
notes: Real open Type: Bug; same gateway-list code path as #52 (this is the 403/RBAC variant, #52 is the 404/no-istio variant). Integration-escalation: needs a restricted-RBAC cluster, not the live cos model.

title: docstrings of _check_daemonset_ready / _check_deployment_ready say BlockedStatus but code returns WaitingStatus
summary: In src/charm.py the two readiness helpers _check_daemonset_ready (line 273-274) and _check_deployment_ready (line 286-287) both carry the docstring "Check if a <X> is ready. Returns BlockedStatus if not ready, None otherwise." but the implementations actually return ops.WaitingStatus (lines 281 and 294), never BlockedStatus. The docstrings are misleading about the function contract.
why_good_first_issue: Pure docstring fix in two adjacent one-line docstrings; no behaviour change. The correct value is unambiguous and is already asserted by the existing unit test (tests/unit/test_status.py:25 expects ops.WaitingStatus). Done = both docstrings say WaitingStatus. No runtime/deploy needed; lint+unit already green.
affected_files: src/charm.py:274 (_check_daemonset_ready docstring); src/charm.py:287 (_check_deployment_ready docstring); evidence of intended behaviour: src/charm.py:281,294 (return ops.WaitingStatus) and tests/unit/test_status.py:25 (asserts ops.WaitingStatus)
repro_steps: multipass exec cos -- bash -lc 'cd ~/work/istio-k8s-operator && sed -n "272,295p" src/charm.py' ; multipass exec cos -- bash -lc 'cd ~/work/istio-k8s-operator && sed -n "15,28p" tests/unit/test_status.py' ; multipass exec cos -- bash -lc 'cd ~/work/istio-k8s-operator && export PATH=$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH && tox -e unit'
repro_result: Confirmed by inspection + unit run. src/charm.py:274 docstring='Check if a DaemonSet is ready. Returns BlockedStatus if not ready, None otherwise.' yet src/charm.py:281 returns ops.WaitingStatus(message); same mismatch at src/charm.py:287 vs :294. The intended return is WaitingStatus, proven by tests/unit/test_status.py:25 'assert state.unit_status == ops.WaitingStatus("Istio CNI not ready. Possible platform mismatch. Check charm config.")'. tox -e unit PASSED (exit 0) and tox -e lint passed (All checks passed!), so docstring is the only defect. Observed: docstring says BlockedStatus; Expected: WaitingStatus.

title: Missing RBAC permissions when deploying istio-k8s operator in the Charmed Kubernetes
summary: When deployed on Charmed Kubernetes, istio-k8s fails with lightkube ApiError: 'customresourcedefinitions.apiextensions.k8s.io is forbidden: User system:serviceaccount:istio-system:istio-k8s cannot list resource customresourcedefinitions ... at the cluster scope'. The charm uses lightkube to get/list/patch cluster-scoped resources (CustomResourceDefinition, ClusterRole, ClusterRoleBinding are imported in src/charm.py:51,56-58) but the required ClusterRole/ClusterRoleBinding granting list/get/patch on customresourcedefinitions is not provisioned, so it works on MicroK8s (broad RBAC) but not on Charmed Kubernetes.
why_good_first_issue: Well-scoped and the reporter already identified the exact missing verbs (list, get, patch on customresourcedefinitions) and the fix shape (add ClusterRole + ClusterRoleBinding). A beginner can add the RBAC rules and validate against the error message. Slightly above trivial because it needs a Charmed-Kubernetes cluster to fully verify, hence integration tier.
affected_files: src/charm.py:51 (imports CustomResourceDefinition), src/charm.py:56-58,74-75 (ClusterRole/ClusterRoleBinding usage); charmcraft.yaml (charm-level RBAC / no cluster-scope grant for CRDs); src/manifests/gateway-apis-crds.yaml
repro_steps: PLAN (do NOT run on the live cos VM): 1) On a Charmed-Kubernetes substrate (NOT MicroK8s, whose default RBAC is permissive): juju add-model istio-system; juju deploy istio-k8s --trust --channel 2/edge. 2) Observe unit go to error; juju debug-log shows lightkube.core.exceptions.ApiError 'customresourcedefinitions.apiextensions.k8s.io is forbidden ... cannot list resource customresourcedefinitions ... at the cluster scope'. 3) Confirm fix: add ClusterRole/ClusterRoleBinding granting [list,get,patch] on apiextensions.k8s.io/customresourcedefinitions and re-deploy; unit should reach active.
repro_result: LIVE-ATTEMPTED 2026-06-16: NOT reproducible on microk8s (istio-k8s deployed without --trust still went active). Needs Charmed Kubernetes' stricter RBAC. || needs-live-deploy (flagged for orchestrator). Could not reproduce locally: MicroK8s on the shared cos VM has permissive RBAC and the repo is read-only; the bug only manifests on Charmed Kubernetes per the issue. Static evidence supporting the report: src/charm.py imports and operates on cluster-scoped CustomResourceDefinition (line 51) and ClusterRole/ClusterRoleBinding (lines 56-58, 74-75) via lightkube_client, matching the forbidden 'list customresourcedefinitions' error in the issue.
fix_hint: Add a ClusterRole (rules: apiGroups ['apiextensions.k8s.io'], resources ['customresourcedefinitions'], verbs ['get','list','patch']) plus a ClusterRoleBinding to the istio-k8s service account, provisioned by the charm (or documented as a required --trust/manifest). Cross-check other cluster-scoped resources the charm touches and grant matching verbs.
notes: Real open Type: Bug issue. Integration-escalation: requires a Charmed Kubernetes deployment that the shared live cos model must not be used for. Reporter pinned the exact missing verbs, which de-risks the fix.

title: Wrong bind address in docstring: says 127.0.0.1:6000 but service listens on all interfaces (:6000)
suggested_labels: good first issue, Type: Documentation, Type: Bug
summary: JujuIntrospect.install's docstring (src/jujuintrospect.py:22) states 'The systemd unit will start juju-introspect listening on 127.0.0.1:6000'. The actual unit file src/configs/juju-introspect.service:9 runs `ExecStart=/usr/bin/juju-introspect --listen=:6000`, i.e. it binds to ALL interfaces (0.0.0.0:6000), not loopback. The docstring is factually wrong and is also self-contradictory with the charm's purpose: it calls `open-port 6000/TCP` (src/charm.py:52) and advertises a Parca scrape target at *:6000 (src/charm.py:30) for REMOTE profiling, which only works because the service is not bound to 127.0.0.1.
why_good_first_issue: One-line docstring correction with an unambiguous source of truth (the .service file). No behaviour change, no test change. Teaches a newcomer to cross-check docstrings against the actual systemd ExecStart and to understand `--listen=:PORT` vs `--listen=127.0.0.1:PORT`. Verifiable purely by reading two files.
repro_steps: grep -n 'listen\|127.0.0.1\|6000' src/jujuintrospect.py src/configs/juju-introspect.service. Observe docstring at src/jujuintrospect.py:22 claims 127.0.0.1:6000 while src/configs/juju-introspect.service:9 uses `--listen=:6000` (empty host => all interfaces).
repro_result: Confirmed at current HEAD. src/jujuintrospect.py:22 = 'The systemd unit will start juju-introspect listening on 127.0.0.1:6000'. src/configs/juju-introspect.service:9 = 'ExecStart=/usr/bin/juju-introspect --listen=:6000'. `--listen=:6000` binds to 0.0.0.0, contradicting the docstring. README.md:5 and charmcraft.yaml:6 only say 'port 6000' (no host), so they are fine; only the code docstring is wrong. Unit suite green (5 passed, tox -e unit), unaffected since this is docs-only.
fix_hint: Change src/jujuintrospect.py:22 to say the unit listens on port 6000 on all interfaces (e.g. '...listening on :6000 (all interfaces)') to match --listen=:6000. Do NOT 'fix' it the other way by adding 127.0.0.1 to the unit file -- that would break remote scraping, which is the charm's purpose.
notes: Machine/systemd charm; 0 open issues -> self-identified. ops via ops.main. Unit env builds and passes (5 tests; charm.py 100% covered). Note the .service file also has trailing whitespace on most lines (cosmetic) if a contributor wants a tiny extra cleanup.

title: install() copies the systemd unit file but never calls daemon_reload()
summary: JujuIntrospect.install (src/jujuintrospect.py:23-24) does shutil.copy('src/configs/juju-introspect.service', self.SVC_PATH) to write a new systemd unit file, but never runs `systemd daemon-reload` afterwards. systemd only picks up new/changed unit files after a daemon-reload; relying on the subsequent service_resume (start, src/jujuintrospect.py:34) to notice the unit is fragile and version-dependent. The operator_libs_linux v1 systemd lib already provides daemon_reload() (lib/charms/operator_libs_linux/v1/systemd.py:279), so the fix is small. remove() (src/jujuintrospect.py:27-30) similarly deletes the unit file without a daemon-reload.
why_good_first_issue: Localised fix in a tiny helper class: import is already present (from charms.operator_libs_linux.v1 import systemd) and the needed function exists. Add systemd.daemon_reload() after the copy in install() (and optionally after the remove in remove()). It's a well-known charm best practice for managing systemd units, so it is a good slightly-above-trivial first issue. A matching unit test can assert daemon_reload is called.
affected_files: src/jujuintrospect.py:23-24 (install: shutil.copy with no daemon_reload); src/jujuintrospect.py:27-30 (remove: os.remove with no daemon_reload); helper available at lib/charms/operator_libs_linux/v1/systemd.py:279 (daemon_reload)
repro_steps: grep -rn 'daemon_reload' src/ -> no matches (the charm never reloads systemd). grep -n 'def daemon_reload' lib/charms/operator_libs_linux/v1/systemd.py -> exists at line 279. Inspect src/jujuintrospect.py:install() -> copy then nothing. Baseline: export PATH=$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH && tox -e unit.
repro_result: Confirmed at current HEAD. `grep -rn daemon_reload src/` returns nothing, so the charm writes/removes /etc/systemd/system/juju-introspect.service (src/jujuintrospect.py:16,23-24,27-30) without ever calling daemon-reload, while daemon_reload() is available (systemd.py:279). Unit coverage marks src/jujuintrospect.py:24 (install body) as Missing, i.e. only exercised at functional/integration tier; unit baseline 5 passed (tox -e unit), charm.py 100%, jujuintrospect.py 52%.
fix_hint: In install(), after shutil.copy(...), call systemd.daemon_reload(). Consider also calling it in remove() after os.remove(self.SVC_PATH). Add/extend a unit test that patches charms.operator_libs_linux.v1.systemd and asserts daemon_reload() is invoked. (The functional test in tests/functional can validate the real reload.)
notes: Machine/systemd charm; 0 open issues -> self-identified. Slightly higher difficulty than the docstring fix because it touches behaviour and benefits from a test, hence 'medium'. Real correctness concern: without daemon-reload, a changed unit file may not be honoured on upgrade. Flagged as unit-tier because the missing call is provable in code + a unit test can cover the fix; full end-to-end validation is the functional/LXD env (tox -e functional), which this run did not execute.

title: LokiRequestLatency alert title mangled
suggested_labels: Type: Bug; Priority: High; good first issue
summary: The LokiRequestLatency alert renders 'Loki request latency (instance ) (LokiRequestLatency)' with an empty instance. The rule's summary references {{ $labels.instance }} but its PromQL expr aggregates with `sum(...) by (le)`, which discards every label except `le` (including instance, job, route). So $labels.instance (and $labels.job/$labels.route used in the description) are empty when the alert fires.
why_good_first_issue: Single rule file, root cause provable by reading the rule, and a small contained fix (either drop the {{ $labels.instance }} from the summary or preserve the needed labels in the `by (...)` grouping). No charm code or live cluster needed to understand it.
repro_steps: multipass exec cos -- bash -lc 'cd ~/work/loki-k8s-operator && cat -n src/prometheus_alert_rules/loki_request_latency.rule'
repro_result: Confirmed by inspection: line 6 expr = `(histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(loki_request_duration_seconds_bucket{route!~"(?i).*tail.*"}[5m])) by (le))) > 1` -- aggregation is `by (le)` only. Line 11 summary = `Loki request latency (instance {{ $labels.instance }})`. Because `by (le)` drops the instance label, $labels.instance is empty -> exactly the reported 'Loki request latency (instance ) (LokiRequestLatency)'. Same dropped-label problem affects $labels.job/$labels.route in the description (line 12). Observed-vs-expected: title shows empty instance vs should show the firing instance.
fix_hint: Either (a) remove the (instance {{ $labels.instance }}) fragment from the summary (and the job/route refs from the description) since the aggregated series has no such labels, or (b) preserve the labels by widening the grouping, e.g. `... by (le, instance)` / juju topology labels. Note the same `(instance {{ $labels.instance }})` summary pattern also appears in loki_request_errors.rule:11 and loki_request_panic.rule:11 whose exprs group by (namespace, job, route) and (namespace, job) respectively -- neither keeps `instance` -- so consider fixing all three consistently.
notes: Type: Bug + Priority: High in tracker, still open and reproducible from the rule definition. instance label absent because Juju-topology series do not carry a raw `instance` label and the expr aggregates it away regardless.

title: Refactor `LokiOperatorCharm._check_alert_rules`
suggested_labels: Type: Enhancement; Type: Cleanup; good first issue
summary: _check_alert_rules (src/charm.py:959) is misnamed and does too much: the name implies a file/disk check but it actually verifies rules via the Loki HTTP API; it side-effects by writing self._stored.status['rules'] (BlockedStatus/ActiveStatus) instead of letting the caller manage status; and it returns None rather than a bool. The issue asks for a rename, removal of the status side-effect, and a bool return (True on 200, False otherwise).
why_good_first_issue: Well-scoped single-method refactor with an explicit 3-point definition of done in the issue. Touches one method plus its two call sites (charm.py:679 and :867). No new behaviour, easy to cover with existing unit tests.
affected_files: src/charm.py:959-997 (_check_alert_rules); call sites src/charm.py:679 and src/charm.py:867
repro_steps: multipass exec cos -- bash -lc 'cd ~/work/loki-k8s-operator && sed -n "959,997p" src/charm.py' ; and grep -n "_check_alert_rules" src/charm.py for call sites
repro_result: Confirmed by inspection: method def at src/charm.py:959 `def _check_alert_rules(self):` with docstring 'Check alert rules using Loki API.' (so the name '_check_alert_rules' does not convey it's an API call). In every branch it assigns self._stored.status['rules'] = to_tuple(BlockedStatus(...)) or to_tuple(ActiveStatus()) (lines ~985, ~989, ~992, ~997) and `return`s None -- never returns a bool. Matches all three issue points (name, side-effect, missing bool return). Called at charm.py:679 and :867 ignoring any return value.
fix_hint: Rename to something like `_verify_alert_rules_via_api`/`_alert_rules_ok`; have it return True when urlopen succeeds (HTTP 200) and False on HTTPError/URLError/other; move the self._stored.status['rules'] = ... assignments into the callers (charm.py:679, :867) based on the returned bool. Update unit tests accordingly.
notes: Type: Enhancement in tracker but is really a Type: Cleanup refactor; behaviour-preserving. There is an existing comment at charm.py:671-673 already noting the awkward status side-effect, reinforcing the refactor's intent.

title: Ambiguous requires-python specifier `~=3.10` (no patch version) in coordinator and worker pyproject.toml
summary: Both charm packages declare `requires-python = "~=3.10"` (coordinator/pyproject.toml:5 and worker/pyproject.toml:6). PEP 440's tilde operator without a patch version is interpreted as `>=3.10,<4`, NOT the apparently-intended `>=3.10,<3.11`. uv emits a warning about exactly this on every tox run. Either the intent is `>=3.10` (use that explicit form) or `~=3.10.0` (to pin to 3.10.x). The ambiguous form should be replaced with an explicit, unambiguous specifier in both files.
why_good_first_issue: Two-line mechanical edit in two sibling files, no behaviour change, and the fix is self-evidently correct. The build tool (uv) already surfaces the exact warning, so a newcomer can verify the before/after by running `tox -e lint` and seeing the warning disappear. No charm/cluster knowledge required; great first contribution to learn the repo's tox/uv flow.
repro_steps: multipass exec cos -- bash -lc 'cd ~/work/loki-operators && grep -n "requires-python" coordinator/pyproject.toml worker/pyproject.toml' ; multipass exec cos -- bash -lc 'cd ~/work/loki-operators/coordinator && export PATH=$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH && tox -e lint 2>&1 | head'
repro_result: Confirmed by inspection + tooling. grep shows `coordinator/pyproject.toml:5:requires-python = "~=3.10"` and `worker/pyproject.toml:6:requires-python = "~=3.10"`. Running `tox -e lint` in coordinator/ prints: warning: The `requires-python` specifier (`~=3.10`) in `loki-coordinator-k8s` uses the tilde specifier (`~=`) without a patch version. This will be interpreted as `>=3.10, <4`. Did you mean `~=3.10.0` ... We recommend only using the tilde specifier with a patch version to avoid ambiguity. So the tool confirms the specifier is ambiguous. (lint otherwise passes: 'All checks passed!').
fix_hint: Decide intent: if any Python >=3.10 is supported, change both lines to `requires-python = ">=3.10"`; if only 3.10.x, use `requires-python = "~=3.10.0"`. Given the charms run on modern bases, `>=3.10` is the most likely correct intent. Apply identically to coordinator/pyproject.toml and worker/pyproject.toml. Re-run `tox -e lint` in each to confirm the uv warning is gone.
notes: Not an existing tracker issue; self-identified during repro. Both charms in this monorepo share the same defect. Verified at repo HEAD (depth-80 clone). Trivially confirmable without a live deployment.

title: Loki saves invalid LogQL rule to disk, `lokitool` fails to sync ALL rules because of one invalid rule
suggested_labels: Type: Bug; Status: Triage; good first issue
summary: The coordinator's `_set_alerts` (coordinator/src/charm.py:272) writes EVERY alert rule file received from consumers to disk via `_push_alert_rules` (charm.py:242), then runs a single `lokitool rules sync <all files> --address=... --id=fake` (charm.py:~295-309). lokitool sync is atomic: if any one rule file is invalid, the whole sync fails and NO rules get loaded. The coordinator does no per-rule validation before writing/syncing; it only logs lokitool's stderr and returns (charm.py:~315). Upstream, the loki_push_api provider is supposed to validate rules with cos-tool and only persist valid ones, but invalid rules still reach disk, so one bad rule from one consumer takes down rule-loading for all consumers.
why_good_first_issue: Tightly-scoped, single method (`_set_alerts`) with a clear, well-documented failure mode in the issue. A beginner can add resilience without deep distributed-systems knowledge: e.g. validate each rules file (or sync them individually / skip-and-warn on the offending file) so one bad rule no longer blocks the rest. Good intro to the charm's alert-handling flow and to ops Pebble exec. The exact code path is small and localized.
affected_files: coordinator/src/charm.py (_set_alerts at :272, _push_alert_rules at :242, lokitool sync exec ~:295-309)
repro_steps: Full repro needs a live deploy (issue provides a bundle): deploy loki-coordinator-k8s + loki-worker-k8s + cos-configuration-k8s pointed at a repo containing one invalid LogQL rule (github.com/sinapah/cos-config), relate cos-config:loki-config -> loki:logging, then observe `juju debug-log` shows lokitool sync failing for ALL rules. Inspection of the code path (no deploy): multipass exec cos -- bash -lc 'cd ~/work/loki-operators && sed -n "242,320p" coordinator/src/charm.py'.
repro_result: LIVE-REPRODUCED 2026-06-16 (gfi-repro VM; full loki coordinator+worker+minio+s3 stack): ran the coordinator's exact `lokitool rules sync valid.yaml invalid.yaml --id=fake` -> exit 1, 'sync operation unsuccessful, unable to parse rules files' (one invalid LogQL rule aborts the WHOLE batch, valid rule dropped); valid-only sync proceeds ('creating group valid_group'). Confirms one bad rule blocks all. || needs-live-deploy (flagged for orchestrator). Code-path confirmed by inspection at HEAD: `_push_alert_rules` (charm.py:242) loops over all alert dicts and `self._push(path, rules)` writes each to RULES_DIR with NO validation; `_set_alerts` then calls `lokitool rules sync *rules_file_paths --address=... --id=fake` in a single pebble exec (charm.py ~:296-309). On nonzero/stderr output it just `logger.error(...)` and `return`s without persisting the hash (charm.py ~:313-315) -- i.e. all-or-nothing, exactly matching the reported atomic-failure behaviour. The validation gap the issue asks about lives in the consumer-side loki_push_api lib (hosted in loki-k8s-operator), but the atomic-sync amplification and the practical mitigation point are here in the coordinator.
fix_hint: In `_set_alerts`/`_push_alert_rules` add resilience to a single bad rule: either (a) validate each rules file before writing (e.g. via cos-tool / lokitool check) and skip+warn on invalid ones, or (b) run `lokitool rules sync` per-file so one failure doesn't block the others, or (c) at minimum surface WHICH file failed in the BlockedStatus/log. Note the true root cause (invalid rules reaching disk) is in charms.loki_k8s.v1.loki_push_api in loki-k8s-operator; consider a cross-repo note. Keep the hash-persist-only-on-success behaviour.
notes: Open, labelled Type: Bug + Status: Triage. Reproduction bundle is in the issue. Distinct from #66 (S3 ca_file), which I verified is ALREADY FIXED at HEAD by PR #65 / commit 19b71e1 (2026-05-15): _s3_storage_config (loki_config.py:289-290) now renders ca_file and is shared by BOTH common.storage.s3 (loki_config.py:128) and storage_config.aws (loki_config.py:307) -- so #66 is obsolete and was excluded. #100 (S3 XAmzContentSHA256Mismatch) is an S3-backend interaction, not a beginner code fix.

The change is a file rename plus two string updates in rockcraft.yaml (the organize: mapping and the service command), no build logic. Clear done-state; easy to validate with goss (the /config endpoint). Great first PR.

title: Remove unused `recovery-data` mount point
summary: The worker declares a `recovery-data` filesystem storage mounted at `/recovery-data` (worker/charmcraft.yaml:55-56 mount, :68-70 storage). The only thing that ever references `/recovery-data` is the coordinator's MimirConfig default `recovery_data_dir = Path('/recovery-data')` (coordinator/src/mimir_config.py:114), consumed solely by `_build_alertmanager_storage_config` to set alertmanager_storage.filesystem.dir = /recovery-data/data-alertmanager (mimir_config.py:191-195). But MimirConfig is instantiated at coordinator/src/charm.py:103 WITHOUT passing recovery_data_dir, and whenever S3 is ready (the normal coordinator deployment), `_update_s3_storage_config(mimir_config['alertmanager_storage'], 'alerts')` (mimir_config.py:151) POPS the entire `filesystem` block (mimir_config.py:339-341) and replaces it with a storage_prefix. So in any real (S3-backed) deployment the `/recovery-data` path never appears in the rendered config, and the mount/storage are dead weight.
why_good_first_issue: Small, well-bounded cleanup with an existing tracker issue and an explicit repro. The deletion is mechanical: drop the mount + storage from worker/charmcraft.yaml, and remove the now-unused recovery_data_dir plumbing (or repoint the alertmanager filesystem dir to root_data_dir) in mimir_config.py and its one unit test. Teaches a newcomer charmcraft.yaml metadata, the coordinator config builder, and how to update a unit test -- all without needing a live cluster to understand it.
repro_steps: multipass exec cos -- bash -lc 'cd ~/work/mimir-operators && grep -rn recovery . --include=*.py --include=*.yaml | grep -v /.git/' ; multipass exec cos -- bash -lc 'cd ~/work/mimir-operators && sed -n "44,72p" worker/charmcraft.yaml' ; multipass exec cos -- bash -lc 'cd ~/work/mimir-operators && sed -n "95,120p" coordinator/src/charm.py' (shows MimirConfig() omits recovery_data_dir) ; and sed -n "333,342p" coordinator/src/mimir_config.py (shows filesystem popped on S3).
repro_result: Confirmed by inspection at HEAD. All `recovery` references: worker/charmcraft.yaml:55-56 (mount /recovery-data), :68 (storage recovery-data); coordinator/src/mimir_config.py:114 (default `recovery_data_dir: Path = Path('/recovery-data')`), :119 (stored), :193 (`'dir': str(self._recovery_data_dir / 'data-alertmanager')`); and only the unit test coordinator/tests/unit/test_mimir_config.py:101. Call site coordinator/src/charm.py:103-108 instantiates MimirConfig WITHOUT recovery_data_dir, so default applies; but `_update_s3_storage_config` (mimir_config.py:151 -> :339-341) pops the `filesystem` key for alertmanager_storage whenever S3 is ready, so the /recovery-data path is absent from the rendered config in production -- matching the issue's repro (rendered config shows only /data/* paths, no /recovery-data). Hence the mount + storage are genuinely unused.
fix_hint: 1) Delete the mount (worker/charmcraft.yaml:55-56) and the `recovery-data:` storage entry (~:68-70). 2) In coordinator/src/mimir_config.py remove the `recovery_data_dir` parameter (:114) and `self._recovery_data_dir` (:119), and change `_build_alertmanager_storage_config` (:191-195) to use `self._root_data_dir / 'data-alertmanager'` (it gets overwritten by storage_prefix under S3 anyway). 3) Update coordinator/tests/unit/test_mimir_config.py:101 expectation from `/recovery-data/data-alertmanager` to `/data/data-alertmanager`. Run `tox -e unit` in coordinator/ to confirm.
notes: Open issue, no labels yet but textbook good-first-issue (originally filed under bug template; really a cleanup). Note the issue understates it: /recovery-data is referenced in code via the MimirConfig default, but is inert in production because S3 pops the filesystem block -- so the cleanup must also remove that dead plumbing + fix the unit test, not just the charmcraft.yaml mount. Verified at HEAD (depth-80 clone). Aside: mimir's requires-python='~=3.14.0' is correctly pinned (unlike loki-operators), so no specifier defect here.

title: Some nginx routes are missing (/compactor/ring, /ingester/ring, /store-gateway/ring)
summary: The nginx reverse-proxy in the coordinator does not expose the per-component ring status/healthcheck endpoints `/compactor/ring`, `/ingester/ring`, and `/store-gateway/ring`. In coordinator/src/nginx_config.py only `/ruler/ring` is wired (line 33). There is no `_locations_ingester` or `_locations_store_gateway` block at all, and `_locations_compactor` (line 50) contains only `/api/v1/upload/block/` -- no `/compactor/ring`. So requests to those ring pages through nginx do not route to their backends.
why_good_first_issue: The fix mirrors an existing, working example in the same file: the `/ruler/ring` NginxLocationConfig (nginx_config.py:33). A beginner adds analogous `NginxLocationConfig(path='/compactor/ring', backend='compactor', modifier='=')` entries for compactor/ingester/store-gateway and includes them in `server_ports_to_locations` (nginx_config.py:64-71). Self-contained, one file, pattern-to-copy already present. Easy to add a unit assertion that the new paths appear.
repro_steps: multipass exec cos -- bash -lc 'cd ~/work/mimir-operators && sed -n "20,120p" coordinator/src/nginx_config.py' ; grep -n "/ring" coordinator/src/nginx_config.py
repro_result: Confirmed by inspection at HEAD. coordinator/src/nginx_config.py contains exactly one `/ring` route: line 33 `NginxLocationConfig(path='/ruler/ring', backend='ruler', modifier='=')`. There is NO `/compactor/ring`, `/ingester/ring`, or `/store-gateway/ring`; `_locations_compactor` (:50-52) only defines `/api/v1/upload/block/`, and there are no ingester/store-gateway location blocks. `server_ports_to_locations` (:64-72) concatenates distributor+ruler+alertmanager+query_frontend+compactor only. So the three ring endpoints from the issue are still unrouted -> issue is still valid (not yet fixed).
fix_hint: Copy the `/ruler/ring` pattern (nginx_config.py:33). Add `NginxLocationConfig(path='/compactor/ring', backend='compactor', modifier='=')` to `_locations_compactor`; add new `_locations_ingester = [NginxLocationConfig(path='/ingester/ring', backend='ingester', modifier='=')]` and `_locations_store_gateway = [NginxLocationConfig(path='/store-gateway/ring', backend='store-gateway', modifier='=')]`; include both in the concatenation in `server_ports_to_locations`. Add a unit assertion that the new paths are present. NOTE the issue raises a design caveat (these ring pages have a 'Forget' button) -- a maintainer should confirm exposing them via ingress is desired before merging; flag in the PR.
notes: Open issue (no labels). Issue links the OLD mimir-coordinator-k8s-operator path; logic now lives in coordinator/src/nginx_config.py in this monorepo, re-verified there. Slightly less of a clean GFI than #25 due to the 'Forget'-button ingress design question the issue itself raises, but the code change is trivial and pattern-driven. Verified at HEAD (depth-80 clone).

title: Dead/unreachable log statement after http.ListenAndServe in main (nrpe_exporter.go:210)
summary: In main(), nrpe_exporter.go:206-211 wraps the server startup as `if err := http.ListenAndServe(*listenAddress, nil); err != nil { ...; os.Exit(1) }` followed by `level.Info(logger).Log("msg", "Listening on address", "address", *listenAddress)` on line 210. That final log line is unreachable: http.ListenAndServe blocks for the lifetime of the server when it succeeds, and the only way it returns is with a non-nil error, which is handled inside the if-block and ends in os.Exit(1). So execution never falls through to line 210. It is also redundant -- the same 'Listening on address' message is already logged at line 188 before the server starts. This is dead code that misleads readers into thinking a post-serve message is emitted.
why_good_first_issue: Tiny, self-contained, zero-risk fix in one file (delete one unreachable line). Requires only basic understanding that ListenAndServe blocks. Good for a first contribution to a Go repo: teaches the contributor about blocking servers and dead-code while making a real (if small) correctness/readability improvement. Clear 'done': the unreachable line is removed (and the real 'listening' log at line 188 retained).
repro_steps: Go is NOT installed on VM (GO_ABSENT), inspection tier. On VM cos: clone repo, then `grep -n '' nrpe_exporter.go` and read lines 186-211 (the main() server-start block). Compare the 'Listening on address' log at line 188 (before serving) with the one at line 210 (after the blocking if-block).
repro_result: confirmed-by-inspection: nrpe_exporter.go:206 `if err := http.ListenAndServe(*listenAddress, nil); err != nil {`, line 207-208 logs error then `os.Exit(1)`, line 209 closes the if, line 210 `level.Info(logger).Log("msg", "Listening on address", "address", *listenAddress)`. Since ListenAndServe only returns on error and that branch calls os.Exit(1), line 210 can never execute. The identical message already exists at line 188 (`level.Info(logger).Log("msg", "Listening on address", "address", *listenAddress)`) right after the build-context logs, so line 210 is both unreachable and duplicated.
fix_hint: Delete nrpe_exporter.go:210 (the post-ListenAndServe `level.Info(...).Log("msg", "Listening on address", ...)`). The pre-serve announcement at line 188 already covers it. If the maintainers prefer, the error branch could instead `level.Error(...).Log("msg", "Error starting HTTP server", "err", err)` (currently line 207 logs the message without the err value) -- a nice optional adjacent cleanup.
notes: While reading this file two more low-risk beginner cleanups are visible if a contributor wants more scope: (a) line 207 logs 'Error starting HTTP server' but drops the actual err value (no 'err', err) so the cause is lost; (b) line 71 `ipaddr, _, err := net.SplitHostPort(...)` reassigns err but never checks it. These are optional; the unreachable line 210 is the cleanest single-line pick. Go absent -> verified by inspection, not `go vet` (go vet would also flag the unreachable code).
