title: tox `static` env doesn't run a static type checker (it duplicates `lint`); pyright & codespell dev-deps are unused and [tool.pyright] pythonVersion contradicts requires-python
summary: Three related tooling inconsistencies: (1) the `[testenv:static]` env in tox.ini runs `uv run ... ruff check {all_path}` -- exactly the same command as `[testenv:lint]` -- so the 'static' env performs linting, not static type-checking. (2) `pyright` and `codespell` are declared in pyproject.toml [project.optional-dependencies].dev (lines 16,18) and pyright even has a [tool.pyright] config block (line 50), but neither tool is invoked by any tox env or any local config (grep finds no pyright/codespell command in tox.ini). (3) [tool.pyright] sets `pythonVersion = "3.8"` (line 52) while the project sets `requires-python = "==3.10.*"` (line 6) -- so even if pyright ran, it would type-check against the wrong Python version.
why_good_first_issue: Self-contained tooling hygiene with a clear correct answer: make `static` actually run pyright (and optionally add a codespell step), and align [tool.pyright] pythonVersion with requires-python (3.10). Touches only tox.ini and pyproject.toml; easy to verify by running `tox -e static`. Teaches the contributor how lint/static/dev-deps are wired.
repro_steps: 1) Read tox.ini: [testenv:lint] and [testenv:static] both run `uv run {uv_flags} ruff check {all_path}` (identical). 2) grep -rin 'pyright\|codespell' tox.ini -> no command invocations (only present in pyproject.toml). 3) Compare pyproject.toml:6 requires-python='==3.10.*' vs :52 [tool.pyright] pythonVersion='3.8'.
repro_result: CONFIRMED via inspection. tox.ini [testenv:static] command == [testenv:lint] command (`uv run --frozen --isolated --all-groups --all-extras ruff check <src> <tests>`); no pyright/mypy in either. grep across tox.ini/charmcraft.yaml shows pyright & codespell appear ONLY in pyproject.toml (deps + [tool.pyright]); they are never executed by any tox env or local tooling. pyproject.toml:6 requires-python='==3.10.*' but pyproject.toml:52 [tool.pyright] pythonVersion='3.8'. (Note: pytest asyncio_mode='auto' IS valid here because pytest-operator pulls in pytest-asyncio -- not part of this finding.)
fix_hint: In tox.ini change [testenv:static] to `uv run {[vars]uv_flags} pyright {[vars]src_path}` (so 'static' really runs the static type checker that's already a dev dep). Update pyproject.toml [tool.pyright] pythonVersion from '3.8' to '3.10' to match requires-python. Optionally wire codespell into lint (add a `uv run {[vars]uv_flags} codespell ...` step) or drop codespell from dev deps if unused.
notes: Self-identified (0 open issues). Unit env builds: `tox -e unit` => 2 passed, 82% coverage (Python 3.10.20). Running `tox -e static` today only runs ruff. No outbound writes performed.

title: Dead pytest config: asyncio_mode='auto' set but pytest-asyncio is not a dependency (emits PytestConfigWarning on every test run)
summary: pyproject.toml's [tool.pytest.ini_options] sets `asyncio_mode = "auto"`, but pytest-asyncio is not declared in dependencies or the dev optional-dependencies, and it is absent from uv.lock. There are no async tests in the suite. As a result, every `make unit` run prints: 'PytestConfigWarning: Unknown config option: asyncio_mode'. The option is dead configuration and should be removed (or, if async tests are intended later, pytest-asyncio should be added to dev deps).
why_good_first_issue: Single-line deletion in pyproject.toml with an immediately observable before/after (the warning disappears). No code logic involved; teaches the contributor how the test config and dependency groups fit together. Zero risk: no async tests exist.
repro_steps: 1) grep -in 'asyncio' pyproject.toml uv.lock -> only pyproject.toml [tool.pytest.ini_options] sets asyncio_mode; pytest-asyncio NOT in uv.lock. 2) On the VM: cd ~/work/observability-charm-tools && export PATH=$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH && make unit.
repro_result: REPRODUCED. `make unit` => 16 passed, 1 warning, with the warnings summary showing: "PytestConfigWarning: Unknown config option: asyncio_mode" (raised from _pytest/config/__init__.py:1441). grep confirms pytest-asyncio is absent from both pyproject.toml deps and uv.lock; no async tests exist in tests/unit/.
fix_hint: Remove the line `asyncio_mode = "auto"` from [tool.pytest.ini_options] in pyproject.toml. (Alternative, only if async tests are planned: add `pytest-asyncio` to [project.optional-dependencies].dev and refresh uv.lock.) Re-run `make unit` to confirm the warning is gone.
notes: Toolchain: this repo uses a Makefile (no tox.ini). Unit env builds cleanly: `make unit` => 16 passed, 100% coverage. `make lint` => all checks passed, 6 files already formatted. No outbound writes performed.

title: Unused tool config in pyproject.toml: [tool.black] and [tool.codespell] are configured but neither tool is a dependency or run anywhere (ruff is the formatter)
summary: pyproject.toml declares [tool.black] (line 34, line-length/target-version) and [tool.codespell] (line 61, skip list), but neither 'black' nor 'codespell' appears in the project/dev dependencies or in uv.lock, and neither is invoked by the Makefile (lock/lint/static/fmt/unit) nor by .github/workflows/pull_request.yaml (which only runs make static, make lint, make unit). Formatting/linting is entirely handled by ruff. The black/codespell sections are stale/misleading config that suggests tools that are not actually part of the workflow.
why_good_first_issue: Pure config hygiene: either delete the two unused [tool.*] sections, or (if the team wants spell-checking) wire codespell into the Makefile + dev deps. Easy to scope, low risk, and a good way to learn the project's tooling. ruff already enforces line length, so [tool.black] is redundant.
repro_steps: 1) grep -in 'black\|codespell' pyproject.toml uv.lock -> only the two [tool.*] section headers in pyproject.toml; nothing in uv.lock. 2) grep -rin 'black\|codespell' Makefile .github/ -> no matches (not invoked). 3) Makefile fmt/lint targets call only ruff.
repro_result: CONFIRMED via inspection. pyproject.toml:34 = '[tool.black]', pyproject.toml:61 = '[tool.codespell]'. grep over uv.lock finds neither package (so they are not installed). grep over Makefile and .github/ finds no 'black'/'codespell' invocation. CI (.github/workflows/pull_request.yaml) runs only make static/lint/unit; lint uses ruff (`make lint` => 'All checks passed!' / '6 files already formatted'). The black config (line-length 99) duplicates [tool.ruff] line-length = 99.
fix_hint: Remove the [tool.black] and [tool.codespell] sections from pyproject.toml (ruff already handles formatting and enforces line-length 99). If spell-checking is actually wanted, instead add codespell to [project.optional-dependencies].dev and add a `codespell` invocation to the Makefile lint target + uv.lock.
notes: Self-identified (repo has 0 open issues). Language detected: Python (src/ package observability_charm_tools, pyproject.toml, uv.lock, Makefile). Verified against current main (depth-80 clone). No outbound writes performed.

title: MetricsEndpointDiscovery docstring usage example is invalid Python (broken import + wrong class name)
summary: The module-level usage example in lib/charms/observability_libs/v0/metrics_endpoint_discovery.py (docstring, lines 19-39) cannot be copy-pasted and run. Two defects: (1) the import statement on lines 22-25 is `from charms.observability_libs.v0.metrics_endpoint_discovery import` immediately followed by `MetricsEndpointCharmEvents,` with NO opening parenthesis, yet line 25 closes with `)` -> SyntaxError: unmatched ')'. (2) The example imports a class `MetricsEndpointCharmEvents` (line 23) that does not exist; the real class is `MetricsEndpointChangeCharmEvents` (defined line 101), which is what the example body itself actually uses on line 29 (`on = MetricsEndpointChangeCharmEvents()`). So the import list is both syntactically broken and names a non-existent symbol.
why_good_first_issue: Self-contained, no domain knowledge required: fix a few lines inside one docstring so the example parses and imports the correct, existing class. Verifiable in seconds with a one-line `compile()` check. No tests or runtime behaviour change. This is the canonical 'getting started' example developers copy when adopting the library, so the fix has real user value. Clear definition of done: example compiles and imports names that exist in the module.
repro_steps: On VM cos: multipass exec cos -- git clone --depth 80 https://github.com/canonical/observability-libs ~/work/observability-libs; then `grep -n '' lib/charms/observability_libs/v0/metrics_endpoint_discovery.py | sed -n '19,40p'` to view the example, and `grep -n '^class ' lib/.../metrics_endpoint_discovery.py` to confirm the real class names. Reproduced the SyntaxError by extracting lines 20-25 of the docstring and running python3 compile() on them.
repro_result: confirmed: extracting the example import block and running compile() yields `SyntaxError: unmatched ')' at line 6`. Source inspection: line 22 `from ...metrics_endpoint_discovery import` (no '('), line 23 `MetricsEndpointCharmEvents,`, line 25 `)`. The real classes are MetricsEndpointChangeEvent (l.75), MetricsEndpointChangeCharmEvents (l.101), MetricsEndpointObserver (l.110) -- so `MetricsEndpointCharmEvents` is not defined anywhere. Baseline: `tox -e unit` = 57 passed, 67% cov, ~12s (so no existing failing test covers this docstring).
fix_hint: Replace lines 22-25 with a valid parenthesised import and the correct name, e.g.: `from charms.observability_libs.v0.metrics_endpoint_discovery import (` then `    MetricsEndpointChangeCharmEvents,` then `    MetricsEndpointObserver,` then `)`. Optionally also add the missing `from ops.charm import CharmBase` / `from ops.model import ActiveStatus` imports used by the example body for completeness. Bump LIBPATCH (currently 7, line 65) per the repo's version-bump static check in tox.ini.
notes: Only 2 open issues exist in this repo (#132 'deprecate and archive' and #128 a complex juju resource-patch bug) and neither is beginner-friendly, so both picks are self-identified from code. NOTE the repo is proposed for deprecation/archival in issue #132 (Status: Triage, not yet actioned) -- worth flagging to a newcomer, but the docs fix is still valid against current main. The static env enforces a LIBPATCH bump on any changed lib file (tox.ini [testenv:static]).

title: Remove dead [tool.black] config and misleading 'using black' comment from pyproject.toml (project uses ruff)
summary: pyproject.toml carries a `[tool.black]` section (lines 44-47: line-length=99, target-version=['py38']) and a comment on line 57 `# Ignore E501 because using black creates errors with this`, but black is NOT a dependency of the project and is not invoked anywhere. The dev extra (lines 16-35) lists ruff/codespell/pyright but no black, uv.lock contains no black, and tox.ini runs ruff for both linting ([testenv:lint] `ruff check`) and formatting ([testenv:fmt] `ruff check --fix-only`). The stale black config is dead configuration and the line-57 comment misattributes the E501 ignore to black instead of ruff, which is confusing for contributors.
why_good_first_issue: Pure config cleanup in a single file, no code or test changes, trivially reversible. Easy to verify the claim (grep for 'black' shows only these two lines plus unrelated pip vendored AUTHORS.txt). Good first PR: teaches the contributor where the project's real tooling lives (ruff) while removing misleading cruft. Clear done: black no longer referenced; comment updated to say ruff (or removed); lint/static/unit still green.
repro_steps: On VM cos: clone repo, then `grep -rin black . --include='*.toml' --include='*.ini' --include='*.lock' --include='*.txt'` and `grep -n '' pyproject.toml | sed -n '44,60p'`. Cross-check tooling: `cat tox.ini` (lint/fmt envs both use ruff) and `grep -in black uv.lock` (no match).
repro_result: confirmed-by-inspection: repo-wide grep for 'black' returns ONLY pyproject.toml line 45 ([tool.black]) and line 57 (the comment); the only other hits are .tox/.../pip-26.1.2.dist-info/licenses/AUTHORS.txt (vendored, unrelated names). No black in dependencies, dev extra, or uv.lock. tox.ini [testenv:lint] = `ruff check`, [testenv:fmt] = `ruff check --fix-only` -> ruff is the sole formatter/linter. Baseline `tox -e unit` 57 passed.
fix_hint: Delete the `[tool.black]` block (pyproject.toml lines 44-47) and rewrite the line-57 comment to reference ruff, e.g. `# Ignore E501 because formatting can exceed the line length`, or drop the comment entirely. Run `tox -e lint static unit` to confirm everything still passes (it should -- nothing consumes [tool.black]).
notes: Bundles naturally with a small related tidy if desired: tox.ini also contains a large block of commented-out legacy [testenv:unit]/[testenv:integration]/[testenv:scenario] config (~50 dead lines at the bottom) that a contributor could remove in the same cleanup PR. As with row 1, note the repo is proposed for archival in issue #132.

title: Missing `title`, `tagline` and `description` configs in `catalogue` in `cos-lite` terraform module
summary: The catalogue application deployed by the cos-lite (and cos) Terraform module is given no default title/tagline/description, so the COS catalogue landing page renders generic/empty branding (issue 'before' screenshot) instead of the intended COS branding (issue 'after' screenshot). In terraform/cos-lite/applications.tf the catalogue module is wired with config = var.catalogue.config, and var.catalogue.config defaults to {} (variables.tf), with no title/tagline/description injected via locals.tf or the module call.
why_good_first_issue: Self-contained Terraform change in one module: provide sensible default catalogue config (title/tagline/description) for cos-lite (and mirror for cos). No provider/runtime logic, no new variables strictly required; verifiable by reading the module. The catalogue-k8s charm already exposes these as config options.
affected_files: terraform/cos-lite/applications.tf (catalogue module block, lines 15-27, config = var.catalogue.config); terraform/cos-lite/variables.tf (variable "catalogue" config = optional(map(string), {})); terraform/cos-lite/locals.tf (no catalogue config/title/tagline defaults); same pattern in terraform/cos/applications.tf (lines 15-27)
repro_steps: 1) Clone repo (depth 80). 2) terraform/cos-lite/applications.tf lines 15-27: module "catalogue" passes config = var.catalogue.config. 3) terraform/cos-lite/variables.tf variable "catalogue": config = optional(map(string), {}) -> default empty map. 4) grep -rn 'tagline|"title"|title *=' terraform/ returns NO hits (only docs/_templates html and conf.py html_title), confirming no default title/tagline/description is set anywhere in the Terraform. 5) terraform fmt -check -recursive passes (exit 0). terraform init -backend=false fails offline because module sources are git:: URLs that need network (cloning loki-k8s-operator etc.), so terraform validate could not be run on the VM; verification is by source inspection. 6) cos (non-lite) module has the identical catalogue wiring (terraform/cos/applications.tf:15-27), so the gap exists there too.
repro_result: Confirmed by inspection: catalogue config default is {} in terraform/cos-lite/variables.tf and no title/tagline/description default is injected in applications.tf or locals.tf; repo-wide grep for tagline/title in terraform/ returns zero matches. Therefore a default cos-lite deployment produces an unbranded catalogue page, matching the issue's 'before' screenshot. terraform fmt -check = exit 0; terraform init blocked offline (git:: module downloads), so validate not executed.
fix_hint: Set default catalogue branding, e.g. in applications.tf merge defaults into the config: config = merge({ title = "Canonical Observability Stack", tagline = "...", description = "..." }, var.catalogue.config), or default var.catalogue.config to those keys in variables.tf. Apply the same to terraform/cos/applications.tf. Confirm exact option names against the catalogue-k8s-operator charm config (title/tagline/description). Run terraform fmt and update the generated cos-lite/README.md inputs table.
notes: Stack: HCL/Terraform. Terraform IS installed on VM (snap), Go is not. Real OPEN issue #189 (no labels). Verified still valid against current HEAD. Consider noting the cos (non-lite) module has the same gap.

title: docs: Incomplete information in Operational concerns (retention & upgrade topics promised but missing)
summary: docs/explanation/operations/index.md advertises (in its html_meta description) 'Operational concerns for COS including data integrity, retention, and upgrade considerations', but its toctree only links 'Data integrity' and 'Terraform module versioning'. There are no pages covering data retention or upgrade considerations. The issue asks to either remove the incomplete promise or add the missing content before publishing.
why_good_first_issue: Pure documentation edit in a single Markdown file (plus optionally adding stub pages). No code, no deployment. The mismatch between the page's stated scope and its actual toctree is easy to see and fix.
affected_files: docs/explanation/operations/index.md (html_meta description vs toctree); existing pages: docs/explanation/operations/data-integrity.md, docs/explanation/operations/terraform-module-versioning.md (only these two exist)
repro_steps: 1) Open docs/explanation/operations/index.md. 2) Front-matter html_meta.description reads: 'Operational concerns for COS including data integrity, retention, and upgrade considerations.' 3) The toctree lists only: 'Data integrity <data-integrity>' and 'Terraform module versioning <terraform-module-versioning>'. 4) ls docs/explanation/operations/ shows exactly: data-integrity.md, index.md, terraform-module-versioning.md -> no retention page, no upgrade-considerations page. 5) Hence the promised 'retention' and 'upgrade considerations' topics are absent.
repro_result: Confirmed by inspection: index.md description promises data integrity + retention + upgrade considerations, but the toctree and directory contain only data-integrity.md and terraform-module-versioning.md. The retention and upgrade-considerations topics do not exist, matching the issue report.
fix_hint: Either (a) reword the html_meta description in docs/explanation/operations/index.md to only mention topics that exist (data integrity, Terraform module versioning), or (b) add the missing pages (retention, upgrade considerations) and register them in the toctree. Lowest-risk GFI fix is (a).
notes: Stack: HCL/Terraform repo; this is a Sphinx/MyST docs gap. Real OPEN issue #250. Verified against current HEAD: only two operations pages exist.

title: Architecture detection uses platform.processor() (often empty on Linux) instead of platform.machine()
summary: get_system_arch() in src/parca_agent.py:26 reads the host architecture with platform.processor(). On Linux platform.processor() returns the same value as 'uname -p', which is frequently an empty string (or 'unknown') because most distros don't populate it. When it returns '', get_system_arch() returns '' (the if/elif don't match), the module-level ARCH becomes '', target_revision (which looks up _snap_revisions[(confinement, ARCH)]) returns None, and ParcaAgent.install() then raises SnapSpecError ('parca-agent snap is not supported for arch=...') on an otherwise-supported amd64/arm64 host. The function's own docstring describes machine-style values (x86_64, aarch64, arm64, armv8b, armv8l), which are exactly what platform.machine() returns reliably.
why_good_first_issue: One-line fix (swap platform.processor() for platform.machine()) plus one mock-based unit test. No architecture or Juju knowledge needed; fully reproducible locally without a deploy. The correct approach is already the de-facto standard in the sibling Canonical Observability code (cos-lib's get_host_architecture and craft tooling use platform.machine()). Clear 'done': arch detection no longer collapses to '' when processor() is empty, and a test asserts it.
affected_files: src/parca_agent.py; tests/unit/test_charm/conftest.py; tests/unit/ (new test for get_system_arch)
repro_steps: multipass exec cos -- git clone --depth 80 https://github.com/canonical/parca-agent-operator ~/work/parca-agent-operator. Baseline: multipass exec cos -- bash -lc 'cd ~/work/parca-agent-operator && export PATH=$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH && tox -e unit'. Then run a tiny repro that mocks platform.processor to '' and reloads src.parca_agent: with unittest.mock.patch('platform.processor', lambda: ''): importlib.reload(parca_agent); print(parca_agent.get_system_arch(), parca_agent.ARCH); ParcaAgent('parca-agent', {'remote-store-address':'x'}, set()).target_revision. Run via: PYTHONPATH=.:lib:src uv run --frozen --isolated --extra=dev python repro.py.
repro_result: confirmed: baseline tox -e unit = 22 passed, 76% coverage in ~6s. On the cos VM platform.processor()=='x86_64' and platform.machine()=='x86_64', but when platform.processor() is patched to '' (its documented common Linux value) get_system_arch() returns '' and module ARCH=='', so ParcaAgent(...).target_revision is None -> install() raises SnapSpecError ('not supported for arch='). platform.machine() returns 'x86_64' on the same host, so switching to machine() fixes it. Observed-vs-expected: expected 'amd64', got '' (and an install failure) whenever processor() is empty.
fix_hint: In src/parca_agent.py change 'arch = platform.processor()' (line 26) to 'arch = platform.machine()'. Add a unit test that patches platform.machine to return 'aarch64' and asserts get_system_arch() == 'arm64', and one that patches it to 'x86_64' and asserts 'amd64'. Optionally reuse cos-lib's get_host_architecture helper if/when it is published, to avoid duplicating the alias map.
notes: No existing issue/PR references arch/processor/machine detection (gh issue & pr search returned empty). The two OPEN issues in this repo (#110 and #41) are both the identical automated 'chore: update libraries to new major versions' (update charms.operator_libs_linux.v1.snap to v2); a major-version lib bump can carry breaking API changes and is not a clean beginner task, so both picks here are self-identified.

title: parse_version() docstring references 'parca --version' but the command run is 'parca-agent --version'
summary: parse_version() in src/parca_agent.py:196 has the docstring "Parse the output of 'parca --version' and return a representative string." but this is the parca-AGENT operator, and the only caller (ParcaAgent.version, src/parca_agent.py:179) runs check_output(['parca-agent', '--version']). The docstring names the wrong binary ('parca' instead of 'parca-agent'), which is misleading for anyone reading or maintaining the parser.
why_good_first_issue: Pure one-word documentation correction in a single file; no behaviour change, no tests to rewrite. Clear, objective 'done': the docstring names the same command the code actually executes. Verifiable purely by inspection.
repro_steps: multipass exec cos -- bash -lc 'cd ~/work/parca-agent-operator && grep -n "parca --version\|parca-agent.*--version\|parse_version" src/parca_agent.py'. Shows the caller at line 179 running ['parca-agent', '--version'] and the docstring at line 196 saying 'parca --version'.
repro_result: confirmed-by-inspection: src/parca_agent.py:179 calls check_output(['parca-agent', '--version']), but src/parca_agent.py:196 docstring says "Parse the output of 'parca --version' ...". The binary name in the docstring ('parca') does not match the command actually executed ('parca-agent').
fix_hint: Edit the docstring at src/parca_agent.py:196 to read "Parse the output of 'parca-agent --version' and return a representative string."
notes: Found while reading src/parca_agent.py for the arch bug above. Lower-priority cleanup candidate in the same file (not picked, needs a judgement call): the TODO at src/parca_agent.py:73 'is app_name enough to avoid collisions with other charms' certs?'.

title: Inconsistent default for memory-storage-limit: parca_command_line() falls back to 1024MB but the charm/docs default is 4096MB
summary: The charm declares config option memory-storage-limit with default 4096 (charmcraft.yaml:232) and the README states the in-memory default is 4096MB, but parca_command_line() in src/parca.py:208 computes the limit as (memory_storage_limit or 1024) * 1048576, i.e. it falls back to 1024MB whenever it is called with a falsy/None limit. So the function's own default (1024MB) contradicts the charm's real default (4096MB). The magic numbers are duplicated and out of sync; any code path or test that relies on the function default silently gets 1024MB instead of the documented 4096MB.
why_good_first_issue: Self-contained, one-line code change plus a small unit test. Requires no architecture knowledge: just align the fallback constant with the declared default (or remove the duplication by sourcing the default from one place). Clear 'done': the function default matches charmcraft.yaml and a unit test asserts the produced --storage-active-memory value. Easily verified locally with the existing unit suite, no deployment needed.
repro_steps: multipass exec cos -- git clone --depth 80 https://github.com/canonical/parca-k8s-operator ~/work/parca-k8s-operator; then multipass exec cos -- bash -lc 'cd ~/work/parca-k8s-operator && export PATH=$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH && tox -e unit'. Evidence of the mismatch: grep -n "default: 4096" charmcraft.yaml (line 232) vs grep -n "or 1024" src/parca.py (line 208). Confirm semantics: in tests/unit/test_charm/test_charm.py:29 the DEFAULT_PLAN (built with no memory-storage-limit in base_state, so Juju applies the charmcraft default 4096) expects --storage-active-memory=4294967296 (=4096*1048576), whereas tests/unit/test_workload/test_parca.py:23-38 calls parca_command_line with memory_storage_limit=1024 and expects 1073741824 (=1024*1048576); no test calls parca_command_line with no limit to exercise the 1024 fallback.
repro_result: confirmed-by-inspection+unit: baseline tox -e unit = 132 passed, 93% coverage in ~6s (clean tree). Observed-vs-expected: charmcraft.yaml:232 declares default 4096 and README.md:30 documents 'default limit ... is 4096MB', but src/parca.py:208 uses (memory_storage_limit or 1024)*1048576, so calling parca_command_line() with no memory_storage_limit yields --storage-active-memory=1073741824 (1024MB) instead of the documented 4294967296 (4096MB). The two defaults are duplicated and disagree.
fix_hint: Define the default once (e.g. a module constant DEFAULT_MEMORY_STORAGE_LIMIT_MB = 4096 in src/parca.py, or read it from charmcraft.yaml) and use it as the fallback: limit = (memory_storage_limit or DEFAULT_MEMORY_STORAGE_LIMIT_MB) * 1048576. Add a unit test in tests/unit/test_workload/test_parca.py that calls parca_command_line() with no memory_storage_limit and asserts --storage-active-memory=4294967296. Keep charmcraft.yaml default in sync (or have the charm pass the value explicitly so the fallback never diverges).
notes: No existing open/closed issue or PR references these numbers (gh issue/pr search for memory-storage-limit/storage-active-memory/1024/4096 returned empty). parca-k8s-operator currently has only ONE open issue, #507 'Support ingesting opentelemetry-ebpf-profiler profiles' (Type: Enhancement, Status: Triage), which is a feature, not beginner-friendly, hence both picks here are self-identified.

title: Malformed/placeholder docstring on TLSConfig dataclass ("""Model .""")
summary: The TLSConfig dataclass in src/models.py has the placeholder docstring '"""Model ."""' (src/models.py:44) - an unfinished sentence with a stray space before the period and no description of what the model represents. By contrast the sibling S3Config in the same file has a proper one-line docstring (src/models.py:22). The TLSConfig docstring should describe the model (it bundles the certificate request attributes, the provider certificate, and the private key used for the charm's TLS).
why_good_first_issue: Trivial, objective documentation fix in a single file; no code behaviour changes and no architecture knowledge required. Clear 'done': replace the placeholder with a meaningful one-line docstring matching the project's style (mirrors S3Config a few lines above). Ruff/pyright will still pass; nothing to deploy.
repro_steps: multipass exec cos -- bash -lc 'cd ~/work/parca-k8s-operator && grep -n "\"\"\"Model" src/models.py'. Returns line 22 ('"""Model for the s3 relation databag, as returned by the s3 charm lib."""' on S3Config) and line 44 ('"""Model ."""' on TLSConfig).
repro_result: confirmed-by-inspection: src/models.py:44 contains the literal placeholder docstring '"""Model ."""' on class TLSConfig (note the stray space before the period and the absent description), whereas src/models.py:22 on the neighbouring S3Config class is a complete sentence. The docstring is clearly unfinished.
fix_hint: Replace src/models.py:44 with a descriptive one-liner, e.g. '"""Bundle of TLS material for the charm: the certificate request attributes, the provider-issued certificate, and the private key."""' so it matches the style of S3Config's docstring.
notes: Found while scanning src/ for documentation/cleanup candidates. title: Unbalanced backtick in the 'No targets specified' BlockedStatus message
summary: In _on_collect_unit_status (src/charm.py:203-205) the BlockedStatus shown when no targets are configured is built from an f-string that opens a Markdown/code backtick before 'juju config' but never closes it. The resulting user-facing message is: "No targets specified. Please run: `juju config <app> targets='<scrape target1>, <scrape target2>, ...'" - the opening backtick before `juju config` has no matching closing backtick, so the suggested command renders as an unterminated code span. This is the very first status a freshly-deployed unit shows (the charm has no targets by default), so it is highly visible.
why_good_first_issue: Tiny, self-contained fix to a single f-string in one file; no logic change, no architecture knowledge. Clear 'done': the status message has balanced backticks (and ideally the whole command is wrapped in one code span). Can be paired with a one-line assertion in the existing test_charm_blocks_if_no_targets_specified test. No deploy needed.
repro_steps: multipass exec cos -- git clone --depth 80 https://github.com/canonical/parca-scrape-target-operator ~/work/parca-scrape-target-operator; then multipass exec cos -- bash -lc 'cd ~/work/parca-scrape-target-operator && sed -n "201,206p" src/charm.py'. Count backticks in the concatenated message: there is exactly one (the opening one before 'juju config'), so it is unbalanced.
repro_result: confirmed-by-inspection: src/charm.py:204 contains f"Please run: `juju config {self.app.name} " and src/charm.py:205 continues f"targets='<scrape target1>, <scrape target2>, ...'"; the full concatenated BlockedStatus string contains a single backtick (opening) and no closing backtick. Expected: a balanced code span; observed: an unterminated one. Baseline tox -e unit = 17 passed, 97% coverage in ~3.6s (clean tree), and the existing test test_charm_blocks_if_no_targets_specified does not assert the message text, so the malformed string is not caught.
fix_hint: Wrap the whole suggested command in one code span and balance the backticks, e.g.: f"No targets specified. Please run: `juju config {self.app.name} targets='<scrape target1>, <scrape target2>, ...'`" (note the trailing closing backtick). Add an assertion in test_charm_blocks_if_no_targets_specified that the status message has an even number of backticks / contains the closing backtick.
notes: IMPORTANT: this repo's only 'good first issue'-LABELLED issue, #55 'Add CONTRIBUTING.md' (https://github.com/canonical/parca-scrape-target-operator/issues/55, OPEN, created 2025-01-09), is now OBSOLETE: CONTRIBUTING.md already exists in the repo (86 lines), added by PR #68 / commit 1e0fad3 on 2025-02-14; the issue was simply never closed. Its body also asks to 'do it for all other parca charms' - parca-k8s-operator and parca-agent-operator both already ship CONTRIBUTING.md too. So #55 should be CLOSED, not worked on. The other 4 open issues (#54 list on charmhub, #52 documentation page, #48 release a stable revision, #47 add cos-agent integration) are all Type: Enhancement requiring publishing/release or feature work, not beginner code tasks - hence both picks here are self-identified.

title: 'scheme' config option description omits the allowed values (http/https) and the default
summary: The 'scheme' config option in charmcraft.yaml (lines 70-73) is described only as 'Configures the protocol scheme that would be used for scraping the targets.' It does not list the allowed values (http, https) nor state the effective default (http). The charm validates this option (_is_scheme_valid in src/charm.py only accepts 'http'/'https') and will go to BlockedStatus 'Invalid `scheme` provided.' if the user sets anything else, so the lack of guidance in the option description is a real usability gap. By comparison the 'targets' option includes a helpful example.
why_good_first_issue: Documentation-only change to a single YAML description block; no code change and no tests to update. Clear, objective 'done': the description states the allowed values and the default, matching the charm's validation logic. Verifiable by inspection.
repro_steps: multipass exec cos -- bash -lc 'cd ~/work/parca-scrape-target-operator && sed -n "70,73p" charmcraft.yaml' (the 'scheme' description) compared with src/charm.py _is_scheme_valid (accepts only http/https) and _scheme (self.model.config.get("scheme", "http"), i.e. effective default 'http').
repro_result: confirmed-by-inspection: charmcraft.yaml:72-73 describes 'scheme' as 'Configures the protocol scheme that would be used for scraping the targets.' with no enumerated values and no default, while src/charm.py restricts valid values to ('http', 'https') and defaults to 'http'. Verified empirically that the default behaviour is 'http' (with default config the unit is Active and _scheme=='http'), so the option is fine at runtime - this is purely a documentation gap, not a runtime bug.
fix_hint: Update the 'scheme' description in charmcraft.yaml to enumerate the allowed values and the default, e.g.: 'Protocol scheme used for scraping the targets. Allowed values: http, https. Defaults to http.' Optionally add 'default: http' under the option to make the default explicit and self-documenting.
notes: Found while reading src/charm.py and charmcraft.yaml. NOTE: I initially suspected the missing 'default' for 'scheme' might cause the charm to block out-of-the-box, but verified via ops Scenario that an unset no-default string option falls through to the code default ('http') and the unit is Active - so this is documentation only, not a bug. The 'No targets' status-message backtick bug (other row) is the concrete defect in this repo.

title: README documents wrong config option name (bearer-token vs bearer_token)
suggested_labels: good first issue, Type: Documentation, Type: Bug
summary: README.md:21 instructs users to run `juju config polar-signals-cloud bearer-token="<your token>"`, but the only config option defined in charmcraft.yaml is named bearer_token (underscore, charmcraft.yaml:40) and the code reads self.config["bearer_token"] (src/charm.py:32,38). Juju rejects unknown config keys, so copy-pasting the documented command fails with an 'unknown option "bearer-token"' error and the charm never leaves BlockedStatus('no cloud token configured').
why_good_first_issue: Single-character documentation fix (hyphen -> underscore) in one README line. No code or test changes required; definition of done is obvious; new contributor learns the Juju config-naming convention. Verifiable by reading charmcraft.yaml. Zero architecture knowledge needed.
affected_files: README.md:21 (juju config ... bearer-token=...); reference truth: charmcraft.yaml:40 (bearer_token); src/charm.py:32,38 (self.config["bearer_token"])
repro_steps: On the clone: grep -n bearer README.md  -> line 21 uses 'bearer-token'. grep -n bearer charmcraft.yaml -> line 40 defines 'bearer_token'. grep -rn 'self.config\["bearer' src/charm.py -> code uses 'bearer_token'. Mismatch confirmed; the README command is not runnable as written.
repro_result: Confirmed at current HEAD. README.md:21 = `juju config polar-signals-cloud bearer-token="<your token>"`. The only config option is bearer_token (charmcraft.yaml:40); src/charm.py:32 and :38 read self.config["bearer_token"]. There is no 'bearer-token' option, so the documented command would be rejected by Juju ('unknown option'). Unit baseline still green (3 passed, tox -e unit, 4.32s) since tests use the correct underscore name.
fix_hint: Change README.md:21 from bearer-token to bearer_token so it matches the config option. (Optional: maintainers may instead prefer to rename the charmcraft.yaml option to bearer-token to follow Juju's hyphenated convention, but that is a larger change touching code+tests; the minimal first-issue fix is the README.)
notes: Machine/k8s-agnostic integrator charm (provides parca_store). ops 2.23.1. Unit env builds and passes. Note this repo is slated for deprecation (issue #38), but the README is still the user-facing doc until then, so the fix is still worthwhile.

title: if `peer_relations` is `None`, attempting to access `.units` will raise an `AttributeError`
summary: In lib/charms/prometheus_k8s/v1/prometheus_remote_write.py, _push_alerts_to_relation_databag calls model.get_relation() which may return None, then guards with a truthiness check (`if peer_relations`) rather than an explicit `is not None` check before accessing peer_relations.units. The issue (raised from a PR review) asks to switch to explicit None checking.
why_good_first_issue: Single-line, well-scoped change with the exact fix already proposed in the issue body; touches one library function; no architectural knowledge needed; an existing unit test file (test_absent_alert_duplicated_for_remote_writer.py) already exercises the peer-relation path so the change is easy to validate.
repro_steps: On VM cos: git clone --depth 80 the repo into ~/work; grep -n 'peer_relations' lib/charms/prometheus_k8s/v1/prometheus_remote_write.py; sed -n '506,509p' the file to read the exact code.
repro_result: Confirmed at lib/charms/prometheus_k8s/v1/prometheus_remote_write.py:506-509 (HEAD 14e9a3a, LIBAPI=1 LIBPATCH=14). Observed: `peer_relations = self._charm.model.get_relation(self._peer_relation_name)` followed by `{unit.name for unit in peer_relations.units} if peer_relations else set()`. Expected per issue: `... if peer_relations is not None else set()`. The truthiness guard is the only None-style guard of its kind in the file (other guard at line 482 uses explicit `is not None`), confirming the inconsistency.
fix_hint: Replace the `if peer_relations` truthiness check on line 508 with `if peer_relations is not None`. Bump LIBPATCH in the same file (charm-library convention). Optionally extend test_absent_alert_duplicated_for_remote_writer.py with a case where the peer relation is absent (get_relation returns None).
notes: Labeled Type: Enhancement upstream but is really a defensive-coding/cleanup fix. Note: ops Relation objects are normally truthy, so the practical behaviour change is minimal; the value is clarity/correctness against the documented get_relation() -> Optional[Relation] contract. Verified not yet fixed on HEAD.

title: prometheus charm instantiates GrafanaSourceProvider with `external_url`, then reconfigures it to internal_url
suggested_labels: good first issue, Type: Bug, Priority: Low
summary: GrafanaSourceProvider is constructed at import/init time with source_url=self.most_external_url (an external URL when ingress is set), but during _configure() it is updated via update_source(self.internal_url). Because the provider is instantiated on every event while update_source only runs on a subset of events, there can be windows where the externally-advertised source URL is published to Grafana instead of the intended internal URL.
why_good_first_issue: Small, self-contained inconsistency in a single file (src/charm.py); the fix is to make the init value and the update value consistent (or document why they differ); Priority: Low and already triaged; no deep architecture needed beyond reading two nearby code paths.
repro_steps: On VM cos: clone repo into ~/work; grep -n 'GrafanaSourceProvider|most_external_url|internal_url|update_source' src/charm.py; sed -n '251,256p' and sed -n '690,696p' src/charm.py to read both code paths.
repro_result: Confirmed at HEAD 14e9a3a. Observed mismatch: src/charm.py:254 `source_url=self.most_external_url` at GrafanaSourceProvider instantiation, vs src/charm.py:693 `self.grafana_source_provider.update_source(self.internal_url)` inside _configure(). most_external_url returns external_url when ingress is configured (src/charm.py:503-514), so init publishes external while reconfigure publishes internal. A comment at src/charm.py:691-692 explains internal_url is intentional (refs operator issue #970), confirming internal is the desired value and the init value is the inconsistent one.
fix_hint: Change the GrafanaSourceProvider instantiation on src/charm.py:254 to source_url=self.internal_url for consistency with update_source (and the comment at lines 691-692), or add a brief comment explaining the init-vs-update difference if external is intentional at init. Add/adjust a unit test asserting the grafana-source databag carries internal_url.
notes: Issue body is somewhat speculative ('I don't know if this is a bug with a consequence'); good first issue partly because investigating and either fixing or documenting is itself the deliverable. Verified the mismatch still present on HEAD.

title: Fix stale requires-python (~=3.8) in pyproject.toml; charm targets Ubuntu 24.04 / Python 3.12
summary: pyproject.toml declares requires-python = "~=3.8" (line 6), but charmcraft.yaml builds on ubuntu@24.04 (Python 3.12) and the file is copyright 2025. Python 3.8 is EOL (Oct 2024). The stale, malformed specifier also makes uv emit a warning on every tox run.
why_good_first_issue: Single-line change in one file; clear correct value (3.10+/3.12); no architecture knowledge needed; the wrong-vs-right is self-evident and tooling already flags it.
repro_steps: 1) grep -n requires-python pyproject.toml -> line 6: requires-python = "~=3.8". 2) grep platforms in charmcraft.yaml -> ubuntu@24.04:amd64 (Python 3.12). 3) Run tox -e unit and observe uv warning.
repro_result: Observed: pyproject.toml:6 requires-python = "~=3.8". charmcraft.yaml platforms = ubuntu@24.04:amd64 (ships Python 3.12). tox -e unit emits: "warning: The requires-python specifier (~=3.8) in prometheus-pushgateway-k8s uses the tilde specifier without a patch version. This will be interpreted as >=3.8, <4." Expected: requires-python aligned with build base (e.g. >=3.10 or ~=3.12.0).
fix_hint: Change pyproject.toml line 6 to a valid, current floor matching the 24.04 base, e.g. requires-python = ">=3.10" (or ~=3.12.0). Re-run tox -e unit to confirm the uv warning disappears.
notes: Unit suite passes (27 passed) on the VM; this is purely a metadata correctness/cleanup fix. ops resolved = 2.21.1.

title: Use modern ops main entrypoint: replace from ops.main import main with from ops import main
summary: src/charm.py imports the deprecated entrypoint via from ops.main import main (line 24) and calls main(PrometheusPushgatewayK8SOperatorCharm) (line 355). ops>=2 recommends from ops import main; the ops.main.main symbol is the legacy form.
why_good_first_issue: Two-line, mechanical change in a single file; recommended modern API is unambiguous and matches house style across other COS charms; unit tests cover the charm so a regression would surface.
repro_steps: 1) grep -n 'ops.main' and 'main(' in src/charm.py -> line 24 from ops.main import main; line 355 main(PrometheusPushgatewayK8SOperatorCharm). 2) Compare against ops>=2 docs recommending from ops import main.
repro_result: Observed: src/charm.py:24 'from ops.main import main' and src/charm.py:355 'main(PrometheusPushgatewayK8SOperatorCharm)'. Expected (ops>=2, pinned ops 2.21.1): 'from ops import main'. The dependency floor is ops[tracing]>=2.2 (pyproject.toml line 9), so the modern form is available.
fix_hint: Replace line 24 with 'from ops import main'; the call on line 355 stays the same. Run tox -e unit and tox -e static to confirm.

title: No error while setting scrape_interval to an empty string (empty config silently breaks scrape jobs)
summary: Setting scrape_interval="" (empty string) via juju config returns exit 0 and shows no error in juju status, but the empty value is pushed verbatim into every scrape job. Prometheus then rejects the config ('empty duration string') and the affected scrape job silently disappears. Root cause: _prometheus_configurations builds the override dict from self.model.config.items() (src/charm.py:136) and merges it into each job via job.update(config) (src/charm.py:143) without filtering empty values.
why_good_first_issue: Well-scoped single-file bug with a clear repro in the issue and an obvious fix surface (one method, src/charm.py:125-155). No deep charm/architecture knowledge needed; existing unit tests already exercise this method, so a regression test slots straight in alongside test_change_scrape_interval.
affected_files: src/charm.py:136 (config dict comprehension), src/charm.py:143 (job.update(config)); config schema in charmcraft.yaml (scrape_interval/scrape_timeout/proxy_url etc. are type=string with no default)
repro_steps: On VM: cloned repo, added a temp test mirroring tests/unit/test_charm.py::test_change_scrape_interval but with harness.update_config({'scrape_interval': ''}), then asserted the rendered downstream scrape_jobs. Ran: PYTHONPATH=.:lib:src uv run --frozen --isolated --extra=dev python -m pytest tests/unit/test_repro_empty.py -v -s.
repro_result: REPRODUCED (unit). Output: 'RENDERED scrape_interval repr: ""' — i.e. jobs[0]['scrape_interval'] == '' (empty string present in the rendered job). Observed vs expected: observed = empty string propagated verbatim into the scrape job (key present, value ''); expected-after-fix = empty config value dropped so the key is absent (and/or a BlockedStatus surfaced). Baseline suite: 16 passed (tox -e unit). Matches the issue's prometheus error 'empty duration string'.
fix_hint: In _prometheus_configurations (src/charm.py:136) skip empty/whitespace-only string values when building config, e.g. config = {k: v for k, v in self.model.config.items() if k not in [...] and not (isinstance(v, str) and v.strip() == '')}. Optionally set BlockedStatus to surface an invalid empty value to the operator. Add a regression unit test asserting the key is absent when scrape_interval='' .
notes: ops resolved 2.21.x; charm builds on Ubuntu 24.04. Issue still open & valid (labels Type: Bug, Status: Triage); not obsoleted by current code (no empty-value filtering exists, confirmed via grep).

The fix is to add scrape_interval to charmcraft.yaml config.options and wire it into the rendered job exactly like metrics_path/scheme are at src/charm.py:107-112.

In _scrape_jobs, add 'scrape_interval' to the option tuple at src/charm.py:107-110 so 'if value := self.model.config.get(option): job.update({option: value})' picks it up. title: Yaml validation failed, but no error shown in Juju status
summary: When prometheus rejects the charm's scrape config (e.g. unquoted label values, issue #52), prometheus writes a scrape_job_errors message into the relation databag, but prometheus-scrape-target shows ActiveStatus and never surfaces the failure. _update_prometheus_jobs sets ActiveStatus() as soon as the locally-built jobs list is non-empty (src/charm.py:91-92) and never observes/reads back the remote 'event'/'scrape_job_errors' field. Operators only discover the problem by noticing the target missing from the Prometheus UI.
why_good_first_issue: Clear, well-described user-facing bug with a known trigger and a focused fix area (status reporting in one handler). Good for an intermediate first-timer; slightly above 'trivial' because it requires reading the remote relation databag and adding an observer.
affected_files: src/charm.py:81-94 (_update_prometheus_jobs sets ActiveStatus unconditionally when jobs is non-empty); relation 'metrics-endpoint' databag field scrape_job_errors written by prometheus (per issue logs)
repro_steps: Repro plan (needs live deploy): 1) juju deploy prometheus-k8s and prometheus-scrape-target-k8s. 2) Configure a target with an unquoted label value that fails cos-tool validate-config, e.g. labels="dns_name:k8s-cluster-ai-1.maas". 3) juju relate the two. 4) Observe: scrape-target unit shows 'active' while 'juju show-unit prometheus-scrape-target/0' relation application-data contains event: {"scrape_job_errors": "...validate-config... non-zero exit status 1"} and the target is absent from the Prometheus UI.
repro_result: LIVE-REPRODUCED 2026-06-16: prometheus-k8s + scrape-target 2/stable; labels=1bad:value -> scrape-target ACTIVE (no error) while promtool check config FAILED: '1bad is not a valid label name'. Charm sets Active from local jobs only, never reads scrape_job_errors (charm.py:91-92). || needs-live-deploy (flagged for orchestrator). Code-level confirmation: src/charm.py:91-92 sets ActiveStatus() purely on local jobs being non-empty; grep shows the charm never reads 'scrape_job_errors' (no occurrence in src/charm.py), so a remote validation error cannot affect unit status. Cannot reproduce purely at unit tier because the error originates from prometheus' cos-tool writing back into the relation.

Closely related to #52 (label quoting) and #54 (stale data after failed validation).

title: Avoid hardcoding integration endpoints in the top-level Terraform module outputs
summary: The top-level product Terraform module's terraform/outputs.tf hardcodes every coordinator integration-endpoint name as a string literal in its `provides` and `requires` outputs, instead of referencing the coordinator sub-module's provides/requires outputs (coordinator/terraform/outputs.tf). Unlike the sibling tempo-operators module -- which already wires several of its outputs to module.<coordinator>.provides/.requires -- the pyroscope top-level module uses NO module references at all. This duplication is a drift hazard: change an endpoint name in the coordinator module and the top-level literal silently goes stale. This is the same class of issue as tempo-operators#324 ('Avoid hardcoding endpoints in the TF module').
why_good_first_issue: Terraform-only, single-file, mechanical change with an unambiguous done state and no charm/Python/architecture knowledge required. The correct target values already exist in coordinator/terraform/outputs.tf, and the exact desired pattern is demonstrated in the sibling repo (tempo-operators/terraform/outputs.tf), so the contributor mostly replaces literals with module references. Maintainers already labelled the identical tempo issue 'good first issue'.
affected_files: terraform/outputs.tf (top-level product module); reference: coordinator/terraform/outputs.tf, coordinator/charmcraft.yaml
repro_steps: multipass exec cos -- bash -lc 'git clone --depth 80 https://github.com/canonical/pyroscope-operators ~/work/pyroscope-operators'; cat -n terraform/outputs.tf  (all provides/requires values are bare string literals); cat -n coordinator/terraform/outputs.tf  (submodule already exposes provides{} and requires{}); grep -n 'module "pyroscope_coordinator"' -A1 terraform/main.tf  (source=../coordinator/terraform).
repro_result: confirmed-by-inspection. terraform/outputs.tf hardcodes everything: `provides` (lines 21-24) = pyroscope_cluster="pyroscope-cluster", grafana_dashboard="grafana-dashboard", grafana_source="grafana-source", metrics_endpoint="metrics-endpoint"; `requires` (lines 30-38) = logging, ingress, certificates, send-remote-write, receive-datasource, catalogue, workload-tracing, charm-tracing, s3 -- all literals, zero module references. coordinator/terraform/outputs.tf already exposes matching maps: provides{} (lines 5-12) = grafana_dashboard, send_datasource, metrics_endpoint, pyroscope_cluster; requires{} (lines 14-23) = certificates, ingress, logging, charm_tracing, workload_tracing, s3, catalogue. A real drift ALSO exists: the top-level advertises provides.grafana_source="grafana-source" but the coordinator submodule's provides{} omits grafana_source and instead has send_datasource="send-datasource"; coordinator/charmcraft.yaml defines BOTH grafana-source (line 74) and send-datasource (line 85) as real endpoints, so the submodule output is itself incomplete. For comparison, tempo-operators/terraform/outputs.tf already uses module references (e.g. module.tempo_coordinator.provides.provide_cmr_mesh), proving this is the intended pattern.
fix_hint: In terraform/outputs.tf, replace the literals in `provides`/`requires` with references to the coordinator sub-module outputs, e.g. pyroscope_cluster = module.pyroscope_coordinator.provides.pyroscope_cluster, grafana_dashboard = module.pyroscope_coordinator.provides.grafana_dashboard, logging = module.pyroscope_coordinator.requires.logging, etc. As part of the fix, reconcile the grafana_source/send_datasource gap: add grafana_source = "grafana-source" to coordinator/terraform/outputs.tf provides{} (both endpoints exist in charmcraft.yaml) so the top-level output can reference it. Watch the hyphen-vs-underscore key spellings (top-level send-remote-write/receive-datasource vs submodule send_remote_write/receive_datasource). Run `terraform fmt` and `terraform validate` in terraform/ if a TF toolchain is present.
notes: Inspection-tier (Terraform, no unit env to run). Self-identified because the only label-eligible open issue, #285 ('use nginx module from charmlibs', tagged good first issue), is now OBSOLETE: coordinator/src/charm.py:16 already imports `from charmlibs.nginx_k8s import NginxConfig, TLSConfigManager` and no old nginx lib remains under coordinator/lib, so that issue is effectively done and was deliberately not picked. Directly mirrors tempo-operators#324, which maintainers accepted as a good-first-issue.

title: Add missing `description` fields to top-level Terraform module outputs
summary: None of the outputs in the top-level terraform/outputs.tf (app_names, provides, requires) carry a `description` argument, so the module's published interface is undocumented in `terraform output`, generated docs, and the registry. The sibling tempo-operators module documents every one of these same outputs with a `description` (e.g. 'All application names which make up this product module', 'All Juju integration endpoints where the charm is the provider/requirer'). Adding the equivalent descriptions makes the pyroscope module consistent and self-documenting.
why_good_first_issue: Tiny, self-contained, documentation-only edit to a single Terraform file with a ready-made reference (tempo's outputs.tf) to copy wording from. No code, tests, or architecture understanding needed; done state is unambiguous (each output has a description).
affected_files: terraform/outputs.tf (top-level product module); reference: tempo-operators terraform/outputs.tf
repro_steps: multipass exec cos -- bash -lc 'git clone --depth 80 https://github.com/canonical/pyroscope-operators ~/work/pyroscope-operators'; grep -c 'description' terraform/outputs.tf  (returns 0); cat -n terraform/outputs.tf  (confirm app_names/provides/requires have no description); compare with tempo-operators/terraform/outputs.tf where each output has a description line.
repro_result: confirmed-by-inspection. `grep -c description terraform/outputs.tf` returns 0 -- the three outputs app_names (lines 1-17), provides (19-26) and requires (28-39) have no `description` argument. By contrast tempo-operators/terraform/outputs.tf gives each output a description ('All application names which make up this product module' on app_names; 'All Juju integration endpoints where the charm is the provider' / '... the requirer' on provides/requires), so the pyroscope module is the inconsistent/undocumented one.
fix_hint: Add a `description = "..."` line to each output block in terraform/outputs.tf, reusing tempo-operators' wording: app_names -> 'All application names which make up this product module'; provides -> 'All Juju integration endpoints where the charm is the provider'; requires -> 'All Juju integration endpoints where the charm is the requirer'. Run `terraform fmt` in terraform/ to keep alignment.
notes: Inspection-tier (Terraform). Independent of and smaller than the hardcoding cleanup (Pick 1), though both touch the same file -- could be combined into one PR if desired. Self-identified because the open-issue pool is thin/blocked: #285 is obsolete (nginx charmlib already adopted); #271 (remove cpu_limit/memory_limit) is explicitly future-work gated on 'track 3' and cross-repo coordination; #276 is a design-y TF storage enhancement; #315 is integration-tier and blocked on an upstream decision (flagged separately).

title: Change the path of script-exporter-script (move executable out of /etc/)
summary: The user-supplied script is written as an executable to /etc/script-exporter-script (src/charm.py:52), but /etc is for configuration, not executables; the file is written with mode=0o755 (src/charm.py:165), confirming it is an executable. The issue asks to relocate it to a more appropriate location such as /opt/ or /usr/bin/ (or under the charm's own dir). The path is referenced only via the single attribute self._single_script_path, so the change is centralized.
why_good_first_issue: Labelled 'good first issue'. Single constant to change (src/charm.py:52) plus verifying the handful of internal references that all go through self._single_script_path. Clear definition of done; no architecture knowledge needed; unit tests do not hardcode the string so risk is low.
affected_files: src/charm.py:52 (self._single_script_path = LocalPath("/etc/script-exporter-script")); referenced at src/charm.py:112 (cleanup), :165 (write_text mode=0o755), :200 and :204 (script-name handling), :233 (scrape job target)
repro_steps: grep -rn '/etc/script-exporter-script' and '_single_script_path' src/charm.py. Confirm the path is an executable via the write mode at src/charm.py:165.
repro_result: Confirmed in code (current HEAD): src/charm.py:52 defines self._single_script_path = LocalPath("/etc/script-exporter-script"); src/charm.py:165 writes it with mode=0o755 (i.e. an executable placed directly in /etc). Expected-after-fix: the script lives under an appropriate executables/state location (e.g. /opt/script-exporter/ or /usr/local/bin/), not /etc. Baseline unit suite: 4 passed (tox -e unit). Tests do not reference the literal path, so they remain green.
fix_hint: Change the constant at src/charm.py:52 to a non-/etc location (e.g. LocalPath("/opt/script-exporter/script") or under self._script_exporter_dir but clearly separated). All usages go through self._single_script_path so they follow automatically; double-check _remove_file_dir cleanup (:112) and the scrape-job path (:233). Update any docs/config defaults if they mention the old path.
notes: Machine (systemd) charm, not k8s. Issue open & valid; labels include 'good first issue', Type: Enhancement, Status: Triage. ops main entrypoint here already uses the modern form (ops.main(...) per traceback in issue #16), so no ops.main cleanup needed.

title: Failed systemd unit results in charm getting stuck in config-changed hook
summary: If a bad config_file makes the systemd service fail to start, the next config-changed (even one that fixes the config) crashes because _create_systemd_service calls service_restart then service_resume on a service that is not running, and service_resume raises SystemdError uncaught. This wedges the unit: the user cannot recover by fixing the config. The charm also restarts the service in multiple paths (src/charm.py:122, :301, :304) without guarding for a service that fails to come up with invalid config.
why_good_first_issue: Clear, reproducible bug with a precise traceback pointing at src/charm.py:_create_systemd_service (service_resume). Fix is localized (guard/ordering of service start vs config write, or catch SystemdError and surface BlockedStatus). Slightly above trivial due to systemd interaction, so a good intermediate first issue.
affected_files: src/charm.py:278-304 (_create_systemd_service: daemon_reload + service_restart + service_resume); src/charm.py:114-124 (_on_config_changed restarts service); src/charm.py:149-155 (_set_config_file writes config then creates service)
repro_steps: Repro plan (needs live deploy on a machine model): 1) juju deploy script-exporter (latest/edge) with a working config_file. 2) juju config config_file=<broken yaml/command> so the systemd unit fails to start. 3) juju config config_file=<valid again>. Observe: config-changed raises charms.operator_libs_linux.v1.systemd.SystemdError: Attempted to resume 'script-exporter.service', but it is not running (per issue traceback), leaving the unit in error and unrecoverable via config.
repro_result: LIVE-REPRODUCED 2026-06-16 (lxd: ubuntu+script-exporter dev/edge): malformed config_file -> unit stuck 'hook failed config-changed / awaiting error resolution'; unguarded yaml.safe_load at charm.py:186 (a 2nd unguarded point besides service_resume). || needs-live-deploy (flagged for orchestrator). Code-level confirmation: src/charm.py:304 calls service_resume(SERVICE_FILENAME) unconditionally after service_restart at :301; if the service failed to start (bad config), service_resume raises SystemdError which propagates out of the config-changed handler. Cannot reproduce at unit tier because it depends on a real systemd refusing to start the unit; the unit tests stub systemd.
fix_hint: In _create_systemd_service, write config/unit file first, then start the service defensively: wrap service_restart/service_resume in try/except SystemdError and, on failure, append BlockedStatus('script-exporter failed to start, check config') instead of letting the hook crash. Ensure a subsequent valid config can still reload+restart. Optionally use service_enable + service_start instead of resume.
notes: Machine (systemd) charm. Issue open & valid (Type: Bug, Status: Triage). Baseline unit suite passes (4 passed). Flagged integration because the failure requires a real failed systemd unit.

title: SIGNAL_STUDIO_MAX_YAML_SIZE_KB is read without validation; 0 or negative silently breaks config analysis
summary: In backend/internal/api/handlers.go handleAnalyzeConfig parses SIGNAL_STUDIO_MAX_YAML_SIZE_KB with strconv.Atoi and assigns it directly to maxSize with no lower-bound check. It then calls io.ReadAll(io.LimitReader(r.Body, int64(maxSize)*1024)). A value of 0 (or negative) makes LimitReader return EOF immediately, so the body is read as empty and every /api/config/analyze request fails with a confusing YAML parse error rather than a clear configuration error. This is inconsistent with SIGNAL_STUDIO_SCRAPE_INTERVAL_SECONDS in cmd/server/serve.go, which validates the parsed value (n>=5 && n<=30) before using it.
why_good_first_issue: Single-file, ~3-line fix mirroring the existing validation pattern already in serve.go. No architectural change, no ADR needed, easy to add a unit test (handlers_test.go already exists). Clear correct-vs-incorrect behaviour.
affected_files: backend/internal/api/handlers.go (lines 42-49); pattern reference: backend/cmd/server/serve.go (lines 38-42); doc: backend/internal/serverconfig/env.go (line 18)
repro_steps: 1) Clone repo. 2) Read backend/internal/api/handlers.go lines 42-49: maxSize := 256; if v := os.Getenv("SIGNAL_STUDIO_MAX_YAML_SIZE_KB"); v != "" { if n, err := strconv.Atoi(v); err == nil { maxSize = n } }; body, err := io.ReadAll(io.LimitReader(r.Body, int64(maxSize)*1024)). No check that n>0. 3) Compare to backend/cmd/server/serve.go lines 38-42 which DOES guard: if n, err := strconv.Atoi(v); err == nil && n >= 5 && n <= 30 { ... }. 4) Go stdlib semantics: io.LimitReader(r, 0) yields a reader that returns (0, io.EOF) immediately; io.ReadAll returns empty []byte; config.Parse(empty) then errors. Go toolchain not installed on VM (command -v go -> not found) so verified by inspection of source + documented stdlib behaviour.
repro_result: Confirmed by inspection: handlers.go:42-49 assigns maxSize=n with no >0 guard, unlike serve.go:38-42 (n>=5 && n<=30). grep over backend/**/*.go shows no test exercises maxSize<=0 (only refs are env.go:18, handlers.go:42-49). With SIGNAL_STUDIO_MAX_YAML_SIZE_KB=0 or a negative value, io.LimitReader limit becomes 0/negative => empty read => config.Parse fails for every analyze request. Go absent on VM so runtime not executed; logic is unambiguous.
fix_hint: Add a lower-bound guard before assigning, e.g. if n, err := strconv.Atoi(v); err == nil && n > 0 { maxSize = n } (optionally cap with an upper bound), matching serve.go's pattern. Add a handlers_test.go case asserting maxSize falls back to default when the env var is 0/negative/non-numeric.
notes: Repo language: Go (gh api .language=Go). 0 open issues, so self-identified. Go 1.25 per backend/go.mod; backend coverage must stay >=80% per the repo's coverage policy, so include a unit test. Inspection-tier only because Go is not installed on VM and must-not-install constraint applies.

title: Dockerfile/README Quick Start do not expose or publish OTLP tap ports (5317/5318) although the tap is enabled by default
summary: The OTLP sampling tap is a headline feature and is 'Enabled by default' per README. It listens on :5317 (gRPC) and :5318 (HTTP) (defaults in router.go and tap_handler.go). However the Dockerfile only declares EXPOSE 8080 and the README Quick Start only runs docker run -p 8080:8080. A user following Quick Start cannot reach the tap endpoints because the container does not expose/publish 5317/5318, so pointing a Collector at the tap fails with connection refused.
why_good_first_issue: Pure docs/Dockerfile change: add EXPOSE 5317 5318 to the Dockerfile and update the Quick Start docker run example to publish those ports (-p 5317:5317 -p 5318:5318). No code logic involved, trivially verifiable against existing config values.
affected_files: Dockerfile (EXPOSE 8080 only); README.md (Quick Start docker run -p 8080:8080; Features/OTLP Sampling Tap section says 'Enabled by default'); defaults: backend/internal/api/router.go (lines 22-30), backend/internal/api/tap_handler.go (defaultGRPCAddr/defaultHTTPAddr)
repro_steps: 1) README.md Quick Start: docker build -t signal-studio . ; docker run -p 8080:8080 signal-studio. 2) README.md states the OTLP Sampling Tap is 'Enabled by default; disable with SIGNAL_STUDIO_TAP_DISABLED=true'. 3) README config table lists SIGNAL_STUDIO_TAP_GRPC_ADDR=:5317 and SIGNAL_STUDIO_TAP_HTTP_ADDR=:5318. 4) Dockerfile declares only EXPOSE 8080 (no 5317/5318) and ENTRYPOINT runs the server which, with tap enabled, listens on those ports. 5) Therefore a container started per Quick Start does not publish 5317/5318 and the tap is unreachable from the host/Collector.
repro_result: Confirmed by inspection: Dockerfile has a single 'EXPOSE 8080' line and no mention of 5317/5318; grep '5317|5318' across *.md/Dockerfile returns only README.md:56-57 (the env var table) and ADR docs, never the Dockerfile or the Quick Start docker run command. README explicitly says the tap is enabled by default. Net effect: default-on feature is unreachable when following the documented Quick Start.
fix_hint: Add 'EXPOSE 5317 5318' to the Dockerfile and update the README Quick Start docker run example to '-p 8080:8080 -p 5317:5317 -p 5318:5318' (or note that the tap ports must be published). Optionally mention SIGNAL_STUDIO_TAP_DISABLED for users who do not want the tap.
notes: Repo language: Go. 0 open issues; self-identified. Inspection-tier (no deploy; Docker build not run on the constrained VM and Go not installed). Low risk, doc-only.

title: Avoid hardcoding endpoints in the TF module
summary: The top-level product Terraform module's terraform/outputs.tf hardcodes most of the coordinator charm's integration-endpoint names as string literals in its `provides` and `requires` outputs, instead of referencing the coordinator sub-module's own `provides`/`requires` outputs. The sub-module coordinator/terraform/outputs.tf already exposes every one of these endpoint names. If someone updates an endpoint name in the coordinator module but forgets to update the duplicated literal in the top-level module, the two drift apart silently and produce broken integrations. The fix is to wire the top-level outputs to module.tempo_coordinator.provides.* / .requires.* (the file already does exactly this for provide_cmr_mesh, require_cmr_mesh and service_mesh, so the pattern is established).
why_good_first_issue: Small, mechanical, well-scoped Terraform-only change touching a single file with an unambiguous done state and zero charm/Python/architecture knowledge required. The correct target values already exist in coordinator/terraform/outputs.tf and the desired pattern is already demonstrated in the same file (lines 23, 37-38), so the contributor just replaces literals with module references. Labelled 'good first issue' by maintainers.
repro_steps: multipass exec cos -- bash -lc 'git clone --depth 80 https://github.com/canonical/tempo-operators ~/work/tempo-operators'; cat -n terraform/outputs.tf  (see hardcoded literals in provides/requires); cat -n coordinator/terraform/outputs.tf  (see the same endpoint names already exposed as outputs); grep -n 'module "tempo_coordinator"' -A1 terraform/main.tf  (confirms source = ../coordinator/terraform).
repro_result: confirmed-by-inspection. terraform/outputs.tf hardcodes endpoint strings: in `provides`, line 19 tempo_cluster="tempo-cluster", line 20 grafana_dashboard="grafana-dashboard", line 21 grafana_source="grafana-source", line 22 metrics_endpoint="metrics-endpoint", line 24 tracing="tracing"; in `requires`, lines 31-36 hardcode logging/ingress/certificates/send-remote-write/receive-datasource/catalogue. coordinator/terraform/outputs.tf ALREADY exports all of these: provides{} (lines 7-12) contains grafana_dashboard, grafana_source, metrics_endpoint, provide_cmr_mesh, tempo_cluster, tracing; requires{} (lines 17-28) contains certificates, ingress, logging, send_remote_write, receive_datasource, require_cmr_mesh, service_mesh, catalogue. Proof the maintainers already use the non-hardcoded pattern: terraform/outputs.tf line 23 uses module.tempo_coordinator.provides.provide_cmr_mesh and lines 37-38 use module.tempo_coordinator.requires.require_cmr_mesh / .service_mesh. So the duplicated literals are exactly the drift risk the issue describes.
fix_hint: In terraform/outputs.tf, replace the hardcoded string literals in `provides` and `requires` with references to the coordinator sub-module outputs, e.g. tempo_cluster = module.tempo_coordinator.provides.tempo_cluster, grafana_dashboard = module.tempo_coordinator.provides.grafana_dashboard, ... and logging = module.tempo_coordinator.requires.logging, ingress = module.tempo_coordinator.requires.ingress, etc. Mind the key-name spelling difference: top-level uses send-remote-write/receive-datasource (hyphens) while the sub-module output keys are send_remote_write/receive_datasource (underscores) -- map them accordingly. Run `terraform fmt` and `terraform validate` in terraform/ if a TF toolchain is available.
notes: Inspection-tier (Terraform, no unit env to run). One first-time contributor (vraj2131) asked to be assigned in a comment but there is no assignee shown and the issue is still open + 'good first issue'; note possible soft-claim. Of the 23 open issues, the cleanest beginner picks are #324 and #257; #272 (Wrong LogQL) is also good-first-issue but is already assigned and being worked on by vraj2131, so it was deliberately not picked.

title: Annoying tracing lib warning: charm tracing buffer exceeds max history length (100 events)
summary: The deprecated charm_tracing charm library logs a WARNING ('charm tracing buffer exceeds max history length (%d events)') every time its in-memory span buffer overflows past max_event_history_length. On charms that ship this lib but run WITHOUT a COS tracing relation (the common case), the buffer fills up over normal operation and emits this WARNING repeatedly into juju-debug-log, creating log noise for an entirely benign/expected condition. Reported against the PostgreSQL charm. The maintainer agreed the level should be lowered and that a new lib revision can be released.
why_good_first_issue: Single-line severity change in one well-isolated function (_prune) with a clear definition of done (no more spurious WARNING; lower level such as info/debug). Requires no architecture knowledge -- it is a logging-level adjustment plus the routine LIBPATCH bump that all ops charmlib edits require. Maintainer pre-approved the direction and a revision release in the issue comments. Labelled 'good first issue'.
repro_steps: multipass exec cos -- bash -lc 'git clone --depth 80 https://github.com/canonical/tempo-operators ~/work/tempo-operators'; grep -rn 'buffer exceeds max history length' --include=*.py .  (locate the log call); sed -n '210,235p' coordinator/lib/charms/tempo_coordinator_k8s/v0/charm_tracing.py  (see logger.warning in _prune); grep -n 'LIBPATCH\|deprecated' coordinator/lib/charms/tempo_coordinator_k8s/v0/charm_tracing.py  (current revision + deprecation).
repro_result: confirmed-by-inspection. The offending call is in _prune() at coordinator/lib/charms/tempo_coordinator_k8s/v0/charm_tracing.py lines 225-228: when overflow > 0 it executes logger.warning("charm tracing buffer exceeds max history length (%d events)", self._max_event_history_length). This is the exact string from the bug report and fires on every prune once the buffer exceeds the default 100-event history, which is normal when no tracing backend is consuming spans. Library metadata: LIBAPI=0, LIBPATCH=13; the file is explicitly marked deprecated ('this library is deprecated and will be discontinued in 27.04', line 7) but the maintainer comment on the issue confirms a new revision can still be cut for this.
fix_hint: Lower the severity of the buffer-overflow log in _prune (charm_tracing.py ~line 225) from logger.warning(...) to logger.info(...) or logger.debug(...) -- debug matches the surrounding dev_logger.debug style used elsewhere in the buffer code. Then bump LIBPATCH from 13 to 14 (do not change LIBAPI). Note the lib stays deprecated; the maintainer suggested new code should migrate to ops[tracing], so scope this PR strictly to the level change + LIBPATCH bump. The charmlib is vendored from tempo_coordinator_k8s, so the canonical source for the edit is this coordinator/ copy.
notes: Inspection-tier: the change is a one-line log-level edit in a charmlib; running the unit suite is not needed to evidence it (the gate that matters for charmlib edits is the LIBPATCH bump, which a contributor must include). The warning originates in the tempo_coordinator_k8s charm_tracing lib but the user-visible symptom appears in any charm vendoring it (e.g. PostgreSQL). Maintainer (PietroPasotti) explicitly OK'd lowering the level and releasing a new revision.
